On further investigation it turns out that my original statements were actually wrong/incomplete. On Linux, TCP_NOTSENT_LOWAT *does* actually affect `select` and `send` and all their friends equally. Even though I gave an example in my first message that seemed to demonstrate that this was not true. According to my improved (but no doubt still imperfect) understanding, I believe that what happened is:

* The SIOCOUTQ ioctl I used to check the buffer size is documented to tell us how much unsent data there is in the buffer. But it doesn't, it actually tells us how much unsent+unacknowledged data there is. So when I was able to write to a buffer that had 131366 bytes in it despite having TCP_NOTSENT_LOWAT set to 128000, that's because the 131366 included some unacknowledged data that TCP_NOTSENT_LOWAT was ignoring. ([I filed a bug to at least hopefully get the man page fixed at least](https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=178241))

* There *is* one case where `select` and `send` disagree. It has nothing to do with TCP_NOTSENT_LOWAT, it just happened by accident to kick in at exactly the same time that TCP_NOTSENT_LOWAT would have kicked in if SIOCOUTQ had been telling the truth. The situation where they disagree is if the buffer is between 66% and 100% full, then `select` says its not writeable, but `send` will still succeed. (The point of this is to avoid waking up processes over and over every time the buffer drops to 99% full.) This is sort of irrelevant to us, just an interesting/confusing fact.

However! AFAICT, on macOS, it *is* actually true that TCP_NOTSENT_LOWAT affects `select`-and-friends, but does not affect `send`-and-friends. This is based on source code reading, not actual testing, so I could be wrong, but I think it's true. It's also a total coincidence that my misunderstanding of Linux turned out to be correct for macOS, since I didn't look at macOS at all when first investigating this...

Anyway, none of this really affects any of the conclusions here, I just wanted to correct that misinformation in case someone stumbles across this thread in the future.

To give some context there are 4 release channels: `edge`, `beta`, `candidate`, `stable`. Differences are explained [here](https://docs.snapcraft.io/reference/channels#risk-level).

As far as I know you there are following strategies:
- use https://build.snapcraft.io to release new version for every commit to channel `egde` and handle other channels manually (you can add collaborators via dashboard) or [via CI/launchpad](https://docs.snapcraft.io/reference/channels#risk-level)
- use [CI/launchpad](https://docs.snapcraft.io/reference/channels#risk-level) to handle `edge`, rest just like above
- release new versions via CI for tagged commit or do it by hand

#### Heres the implementation I think would work best. Suggestions would be welcome:

1. do a $PATH walk and store all executable binaries on a CSV file with a timestamp.
    - include ```alias``` names in CSV list
2. on ```fuck```, check if the timestamp is older than some config variable (default 7 days.)
3. if it is older, then rewalk the $PATH.
4. if current, calculate the minimum Lavenstein distance from the CSV list of commands 
    - if, while running through the commands, the current minimum is 1 insertion/deletion/modification, then return that command
5. either ask the user or just run the command

Hi @maracuja-juice!  From the error message you posted, it looks like `thefuck` is not on your path environment variable.

You can verify that the path is missing by running this command from PowerShell

```powershell
echo $ENV:Path
```

Based on your example, you should see the path `C:\Users\MyUserName\AppData\Local\Programs\Python\Python36-32\Scripts` listed (be sure it has semi-colon `;` separators).

You can also verify that `thefuck` is / isn't on your path by running this command from PowerShell

```powershell
Get-Command -Name thefuck
```

If you get an entry back from `Get-Command`, the tool is on your path, if you get a `CommandNotFoundException` error message, then you'll need to update your environment variables to add it. You can follow the direction on [this stackoverflow question](https://superuser.com/questions/949560/how-do-i-set-system-environment-variables-in-windows-10) to add `C:\Users\MyUserName\AppData\Local\Programs\Python\Python36-32\Scripts` to your path (be sure to close and re-open your PowerShell windows to pick up the changed path environment variable!)

  ```

  ```Powershell
  $env:PYTHONIOENCODING="utf-8"
  iex "$(thefuck --alias)"

If you're checking `__class__` you may as well do `type(adapter)` to get the same information. Still, requests-toolbelt *does* monkey patch both `sessions.HTTPAdapter` and `adapters.HTTPAdapter` (I suspect that you import HTTPAdapter here and then the monkey-patch function is called so the HTTPAdapter class here is not being replaced.) So if someone monkey patches first, then imports zenpy then this will be checking the wrong thing (because everything will default to a Google App Engine friendly adapter and you'll be checking against that). I think what you *actually* want is to take a parameter, e..g, `adapter_class` that defaults to `requests.adapters.HTTPAdapter` and you document that you will always replace the `https://` adapter with whatever is passed to that argument. That allows users to specify what adapter they want you to use and you get to configure the retries.

Alternatively, you could provide some function that provides keyword arguments that users should instantiate their adapters with, e.g.,

```
class Foo(HTTPAdapter):
    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        kwargs.setdefault('max_retries', 10)
        super(Foo, self).__init__(self, *args, **kwargs)

import zenpy.utils
f = Foo(**zenpy.utils.get_adapter_args())
session = requests.Session()
session.mount('https://', f)
# pass session into zenpy
```

Make sense?

Hi @jorijinnall, 

The documentation is correct. The method `add_tags` is exposed via the `TicketApi`, and so the following will work:

```python
zenpy_client.tickets.add_tags(id=3, tags=['atag'])
```

Although you could also just modify the `Ticket` object directly:

```python
ticket = zenpy_client.tickets(id=3)
ticket.tags.append('atag')
t = zenpy_client.tickets.update(ticket)
```

The reason your code above works is that all Zenpy objects hold a reference to the Api that created them:

```python
ticket = zenpy_client.tickets(id=3)
print(type(ticket.api))

>>> <class 'zenpy.lib.api.TicketApi'>
```

So the code:

```python
ticket_to_qualify.api.add_tags(id=221773,tags=["account_qualifed"])
```

Is exactly equivalent to:
```python
zenpy_client.tickets.add_tags(id=221773,tags=["account_qualifed"])
``` 

I can see however where the confusion arises and will add an example that shows how to add tags to a ticket as it is no doubt a very common use case.

Hope this helps!

adding a `dump` and `restore` function definitely sounds useful, but I think there's a couple issues with it:

- while dump/restore sounds simple on paper, I think it'll quickly run into issues where some providers require additional data/metadata/parameters when creating zones, data that lexicon will not have.

I think it should be easy enough to write a script to wrap lexicon that can do what you need on a provider-by-provider basis.

`__extend__`  was designed with this precise use case in mind:

```python
@MyStruct.__extend__
class MyStruct:
    x = 42
    def foo(self):
        ...
```

what it does is simply to copy the items of the new class body inside the original one. It's just syntactic sugar for this:

```python
def foo(self):
    ...
MyStruct.x = 42
MyStruct.foo = foo
```

(apart the fact that `__extend__` has special support for modifying also classes defined in C).

I am not sure what "supporting inheritance" would mean. E.g.:

```python
@MyStruct.__extend__
class MyStruct(MyBase):
    pass
```

do you expect `issubclass(MyStruct, MyBase)` (and similarly for `isinstance(...)`) to be `True`? This is simply not possible, because when `__extend__` is run, `MyClass` has already been created and you can no longer modify it.

We could maybe try to mimic some of the effects of inheritance, e.g. by walking through `MyBase.__mro__` and manually copying all the methods we find. However, I am a bit skeptical that this is a good idea, because it opens a can of worms: there will always be corner cases which we can't fully support: e.g., if you modify `MyBase` **after** you run `__extend__()`, the new changes won't be reflected inside `MyStruct`. Also, this will surely break in presence of multiple inheritance and `super()`.

I agree that there are cases in which such a scheme is useful to avoid code duplicating, but I don't think that such complicated/half-broken logic should be put inside `capnpy`. Depending on the complexity of the code, there are alternative solutions which IMHO work equally well:

1. manually call `__extend__` multiple times to get all the methods you need; e.g.:
```python
MyStruct.__extend__(A)
MyStruct.__extend__(B)
MyStruct.__extend__(C)
```

2. write a custom helper **outside capnpy** which walks the `__mro__` and copy the methods as needed. The advantage is that being outside capnpy, you can write the minimum amount of logic to solve your specific needs, without further complications.

First of all, thank you for the PR! However, the point of pyblake2 is to imitate the exact behavior of Python's hashlib to avoid surprises and simplify switching from existing uses of MD5/SHA1 without changing a lot of code or getting unexpected results. This behavior  includes:

* Continuing without releasing GIL if acquiring the lock fails (this is not documented in hashlib, but matches the implementation.)
* Releasing GIL after seeing > 2047 byte block at least once. This is documented in [hashlib](https://docs.python.org/3.6/library/hashlib.html):

> Note: For better multithreading performance, the Python GIL is released for data larger than 2047 bytes at object creation or on update.

I agree with you regarding the block size, and in fact there's a [TODO regarding this in CPython](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/e5681b9822c633c77ddfeb94585d58895e0ecff5/Modules/hashlib.h#L56-L58):

> TODO(gps): We should probably make this a module or EVPobject attribute to allow the user to optimize based on the platform they're using.

So, my proposal for you would be to bring this for discussion with Python dev team, and if they agree, we can implement a compatible (between pyblake2 and hashlib) way to change the block size.

As for other changes regarding GIL release: since this module is mostly intended for use with Python 2.7 to 3.5, retaining the behavior of hashlib, and since it was integrated into Python 3.6 hashlib, I don't want to change the behavior at this stage, and it's better for any new behavior changes to go right into Python 3.7+ hashlib.

What do you think?

from netmiko import redispatch

@Aditya9009 Would you mind sharing a minimal version of `D:\Python FIles\Telepot\telepot_init.py`?

Well, just telling me "Crashed" does not really help me solve your problem.

The thing you said about inline keyboard not responding after timeout, it's a known problem in 8.3. I have fixed it in today's newest release, 9.0. But I have not time to update the docs. The only place you can see how it works is in the [examples](https://github.com/nickoala/telepot/tree/master/examples/callback).

You also mentioned that `timeout=0.1` seemed to fix the problem. That is extremely improbable. `timeout` is not meant to be used that way.

I suggest you try the following *after* updating to 9.0. Note that I changed `MessageCounter` to extend from `ChatHandler` (instead of `UserHandler` in your code):

```python
from telepot.delegate import (
    pave_event_space, per_chat_id, per_callback_query_chat_id, create_open)

class MessageCounter(telepot.helper.ChatHandler):
   ......

bot = telepot.DelegatorBot(TOKEN, [
    pave_event_space()(
        [per_chat_id(), per_callback_query_chat_id()],
        create_open, MessageCounter, timeout=10, include_callback_query=True),
])
```

1. `per_callback_query_chat_id()` allows the inline keyboard's callback query to resurrect the same delegate, even *after* it times out.

2. `include_callback_query=True` tells `MessageCounter` to capture inline query belonging to the same chat, during its lifetime.

Good luck.

I worked on this over the weekend. There's a subtle issue that needs some attention. KIP-62 relies on new JoinGroup api versions. The v2 JoinGroup api supports passing both a session_timeout_ms and a rebalance_timeout_ms (aka max_poll_interval_ms). The session timeout tells the group coordinator how long to wait between heartbeats before marking the consumer dead and forcing a rebalance. The rebalance timeout tells the group coordinator how long to keep the rebalance operation open so that the consumer can complete a poll() loop, commit offsets, and begin rejoin.

Question is how best to configure when we're connecting to an older broker that doesn't support the new JoinGroup api version. My current thinking is that we should require that session_timeout_ms == max_poll_interval_ms when using api_version < 0.10.1, and that we should only use the smaller default session_timeout_ms of 10000 if api_version >= 0.10.1 . This would allow consumers to use the background thread w/ older brokers, though I dont think it would help actual behavior because session_timeout_ms would still need to be configured as worst-case poll() duration.

*As shared in #993, [this](http://askubuntu.com/a/387600/355433) and [this](http://askubuntu.com/a/387600/355433) were the starting points of my research*

-----------------------------------------

> we have to find a Python Lib to grab stats

The [intel-gpu-tools](https://cgit.freedesktop.org/xorg/app/intel-gpu-tools/) library is usually used through a command line tool called [`intel_gpu_top`](https://linux.die.net/man/1/intel_gpu_top) to monitor intel gpus.

The command line tool could be used by spawning a subprocess and parsing its text output from within python.  Alternatively, the necessary parts of the library's [API](https://01.org/linuxgraphics/gfx-docs/igt/) could be accessed in python through [`ctypes`](https://docs.python.org/2/library/ctypes.html).  The `ctypes` option will be less sensitive because it won't break from text output format changes in the command line tool.

But the API doesn't appear to be a very simple to consume.  [Here](https://github.com/mkuoppal/intel-gpu-tools/blob/master/tools/intel_gpu_top.c) is how `intel_gpu_top` consumes the API to extract simple utilization stats.  The library API is used to directly read from certain registers.

Even with the command line tool text parsing option, it will take some research to understand the relationship between the stats that are reported and the stats that Glances needs.  See [here](http://stackoverflow.com/a/38087477/3367144)

Hopefully there is a simpler option that I don't yet know of!

This is awesome!  FWIW, I'm privately working on code that gets rid of all unnecessary parentheses (which is working except for a few corner cases), and this is what I was going to do next.

It would be nice if you could add some additional testcases to show that comments that are not that nicely formed can still round-trip correctly -- e.g. that we believe that a compilation of the reverse compilation will still generate exactly the same string.

I'm not going to complain too hard about the tests, since there weren't any when Berker took over the project from me :)

But I think that it might be better to write most of them so that, rather than comparing the code, they compare the AST before it gets passed to codegen with the AST that we can generate by compiling the code.  That way, when we make fixes like this that make the code prettier (or for example, when I fix the yield properly), we won't need to go and change the test yet again.

This has the added benefit that you don't need to write the code *exactly* like you expect to see it, so it's easier to do the test.  I would essentially convert the ASTs to strings by passing them through the misc.dump function, and then the test runner can hopefully show you small differences if they miscompare.

That API would be fantastic! `chart.apply_template('templates/my-chart.crtx')`. 

Our use case is that we have an analytics application which allows users to create several types of charts (line, bar, horizontalBar, pie etc...). Our UX designers have created a reference PPTX file with examples of how each type of chart should look. At the moment we're recreating the look of each chart as we dynamically build pptx presentations.

For example, certain type of clustered column charts need to have legends, other charts may not need a legend or title for example.

So the ideal workflow is that we save a set of chart templates from our reference pptx file.
Then as we build presentations we can just apply the correct template - based on arbitrary metadata for the chart.

The real benefit here is that our UX designers can create a set of chart templates within the Powerpoint UI and we (developers) don't really need to worry about the effects. We can just say given chart type "x", apply template "y".

One other thing is that it's possible to have chart templates show/hide chart elements. So a chart template could show axis titles or trendlines. 

If that API existed and we could apply templates - would it be possible to manipulate things like chart axis titles even though there's not an API for it in python-pptx yet. 

If it is possible then in theory that opens up a lot of functionality for developers in that they can store/apply chart templates then dive into the underlying xml to update content/text etc...

I'd be happy to help work on this as it's something we feel will save us time and immediate improve the quality of our generated powerpoint files. I need to skill up a bit on openxml but let me know if there's a way I can help.

Anyhow with the format stuff, my take again is.  We have `state_..`, `icon_..` in some modules.  I think this is fine and makes sense to the user.  We could remove all these but currently I do not see an advantage to this.  This situation is very different to the `wifi` multiple formats to single format issue.  But here, these are definitely not formats in my opinion.  Why? Mainly because they do not contain any placeholders, and we are always using `format`

The reality is that currently you can just use the format and ignore the `icon_..` options if you want.

Now with this module imho we should look at passing `icon_..` through `safe_format()` so that we can have colors for them. but that is about it.  Maybe we revisit this again at some point when we have learnt to do this better.

A few last points

> is_connected boolean to is_connected --> '',' ' 

we can just use a boolean when things are fixed

> get_placeholders_list does not work with \?if=connected.

`connected` is not a placeholder so it not being returned is fully correct.

> We leave users to learn this without a documentation.

yes we do need format documentation, but let us get that before 'forcing' the user to have to learn it

You're right about calibre cli.  I was conflating the two related issues: 

- GUI plugins can't run cron/background threads in calibre (as opposed to BG jobs) because the main GUI thread is used as the DB mutex lock (thus the 'Proceed to update' dialog).  (WoG from Kovid.)

- Calibre CLI can run plugin code, but when the plugin is run that way it can't use its usual GUI code.  Both because it can't pop dialogs/interact with the user, and because it only knows how to update DB the GUI way.

I gave FFF a CLI interface similar to the FFF CLI when Calibre introduced plugin CLI with the idea of pursuing a more full-fledged implementation if it was popular.  As far as I can recall, you're the first person interested in using both Calibre and CLI since, so it never went anywhere.

I might not have been clear enough in my original description: This issue is *not* about integer data in the file, it's about float data in the file converted to integer data in memory!

The current behavior is as follows:

If you have a (typical) *float* file with values `[0.2, 0.6, 0.8, 0.1, -0.4, -0.7, -0.3]` and read this as, say, `int16`, you'll get (in memory) the values `[0, 1, 1, 0, 0, -1, 0]`. This does *not* make sense to me.
If, however, you have a (very non-typical, but valid) *float* file with values `[20000.0, 12345.0, -9364.0, -15042.0]`, you'll get `[20000, 12345, -9364, -15042]`. In this case it would make sense, but, as I said, it's not typical (as far as I know).

When writing integer data (from memory) to a *float* file, there is again no scaling. This *may* make sense in some very rare situations, but typically it's not what anybody wants.

Enabling `SFC_SET_SCALE_INT_FLOAT_WRITE` changes the latter behavior by scaling the integer data (in memory) by `2**-15` (for the `int16` example), leading to *float* data (in the file) in the range [-1.0, 1.0) (at maximum, but depending on the data it could be a smaller range within that range). There is no question about clipping the value +1.0, because it isn't representable in the integer format anyway.
This would be *exactly the same scaling factor* as we are currently using when reading from an integer file to float data in memory. Therefore, at first, it seems to be a very good idea to enable this.

Sadly, and that's the whole reason of this issue, enabling the option `SFC_SET_SCALE_FLOAT_INT_READ` does a very unexpected thing: It looks at the absolute maximum value of your `'FLOAT'` file, and uses this as scaling factor when converting to integer data (in memory). Therefore, there is no fixed scaling factor, it depends on the audio data in the file!

I hope this clears up things, the rest is in my original issue description.

> ... our current behavior makes sense to me and I don't see much reason to change it. Do you agree?

No, the current behavior doesn't make sense to me at all.
But I'm not sure if switching both options on is the right thing to do, because one of them does a ridiculous thing.
Switching only one of them on, would avoid this mayhem, but it would also be quite inconsistent behavior overall.

So probably not changing things *is* the right thing to do after all ...?

> In your answer I see you're calling the destroy function but what is the purpose of the regenerate()? Assuming the user logs out and the session is destroyed when the users comes back the user will have to generate a new session. wouldn't regenerate() be double generating the session id?

``regenerate()`` prevents session fixation. This is a larger problem if your sessions are broken in that they allow session IDs to be passed as url parameters. If I remember correctly, this is not possible with Flask, but imagine it would for a second:

1. Mallory (our attacker) generates a session on yoursite.com, for example by filling his shopping cart with an item. His session ID is MLRY.
2. He sends Alice (our user) a link: "Checkout the sale on https://mysite.com/widgets?SID=MLRY".
3. The server now uses the same session for Alice as well, MLRY.
4. After a while, Alice logs in.

At this point, if we do not regenerate the session, Alice will be elevating Mallory's session with her credentials - he can then use her stored credit card info to order things at his leisure. However, if we regenerate the session ID, we get a new one only sent to Alice (note the use of https).

There are other benefits of regenerating the session id. It is also possible to destroy the session and recreate it after login. The difference is marginal; after all, if nothing is in the session, regenerating an empty session amounts to the same.

Question 2 is a lot easier to answer

> Question 2 : if no for Question 1, Is it possible to calculate the number bars or the period between to datetimes with bt ?

No. Because the number of bars between two `datetime` points is by no means fixed. In an intraday scenario and with 1 minute bars (for example), some of the 1 minute bars can be missing due to the lack of negotiation.

> Question 1 : I wonder if it is possible to precise a specific date instead of a period for SumN ?

  - If a *specific date* really means a fixed point in time, it wouldn't make any sense because `SumN` would no longer be a *moving* indicator.

  - If by *specific* something like a *time* point or a a backwards *timedelta* is meant, the problem is explained in the answer to *Question 2*: the number of bars is not fixed. That means the code of the indicator would have start scanning bars backwards to find the point in time.

That doesn't mean it cannot be done. The code below should suffice (not tested). It makes a simplification by assuming that with each new day a new session starts (which for most markets is simply true)

```python

import datetime
import backtrader as bt

class SumFromSessionStart(bt.Indicator):
    lines = ('sessionsum',)

    def __init__(self):
        self.lastdt = datetime.datetime.min
        self.lastlen = None  # just a placeholder

    def next(self):
        dt = self.data.datetime.date()
        if dt > self.lastdt:  # new day -> new session
            self.lastdt = dt
            self.lastlen = len(self.data)

        lsum = len(self.data) - self.lastlen + 1  #  +1 covers the 1st bar after a day change
        self.lines.sessionsum[0] = sum(self.data.get(size=lsum)
```

> Just to add another observation here, if I set different sessionend= for the two different instruments I am importing (ES and SPY), there is no context of timezone without setting tz=. I am also not clear on how the system handles this when backfilling or if it even uses it.

The `backfill_from` source is only used to fill the initial data until the source is exhausted. By the time the `datetime` timestamp gets to the actual data, the timestamp is already in `UTC`-like format (either because a `timezone` instance has been used to transform it or because it is taken at face value when no timezone is present) and simply stored as it is.

> I've noticed this when in backtest and trying to backtest with different sessionend set. The result is multiple passes through next.

This is a design decision. For each tick you get a call to `next`. Regardless of the number of data sources and how many of them have actually produced a tick. Imagine the case in which a trader wants to trade the `DJI` and the `DAX`. The `DAX` has more trading days and those extra days, the trader may still want to access the last value of the data source and that of the associated indicators. If one waited until the `DJI` was ticking again, some of the ticks of the `DAX` would be lost.

Hi @hemangsk, gunicorn is a wsgi container and will just serve http requests only. Cronjobs run as separate process in a separate environment. django-crontab tries to bridge this gap for typical linux systems so that it should just work and the developer doesn't need to understand the details. But as you're running inside a docker container you need to think about that.

If you run gunicorn within a docker container as "the" process (with maybe child worker processes) there's no cron daemon running and django-crontab will not be triggered. You maybe need to start another container from the same image for the `crond` service (the daemon which triggers cronjobs). But then again, you need to understand how docker and cron works.

from flask import render_template as _render_template

def render_template(template_name, **context):
    """
    Wrapper around flask's render template for
    flask-ask to support arrays in templates.yaml. Will
    choose a random item from the array. Useful for
    intent and slot confirmations.
    """
    source = ask.app.jinja_loader.loaders[1].mapping[template_name]
    if not isinstance(source, list):
        source = [source]
    item = random.choice(source)

    ask.app.jinja_loader.loaders[1].mapping['_render_template_temp'] = item
    return _render_template('_render_template_temp', **context)
```

As far as the changes go, please note that mediatype should always be hashable -- `['texts', 'texts']` is not a valid mediatype value. It should simply be `texts`. There are only 9 valid mediatypes: 

```audio
collection
data
etree
image
movies
software
texts
web```

See http://internetarchive.readthedocs.io/en/latest/metadata.html#mediatype for more details on mediatype.

I do think this change makes sense though, because you should still be able to initialize an Item object even if it has an invalid mediatype (e.g. for repairing the mediatype).

@mcaceresb You know how you were frustrated how pexpect took 50ms to send a line? That's actually an [imposed default of the package](https://pexpect.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api/pexpect.html#spawn-class).

> The delaybeforesend helps overcome a weird behavior that many users were experiencing. The typical problem was that a user would expect() a “Password:” prompt and then immediately call sendline() to send the password. The user would then see that their password was echoed back to them. Passwords don’t normally echo. The problem is caused by the fact that most applications print out the “Password” prompt and then turn off stdin echo, but if you send your password before the application turned off echo, then you get your password echoed. Normally this wouldn’t be a problem when interacting with a human at a real keyboard. If you introduce a slight delay just before writing then this seems to clear up the problem. This was such a common problem for many users that I decided that the default pexpect behavior should be to sleep just before writing to the child application. 1/20th of a second (50 ms) seems to be enough to clear up the problem. You can set delaybeforesend to None to return to the old behavior.

I set the delay to `None` for now to at least try it out. Granted it makes way less of a difference now that I'm only sending a single command to the child at a time instead of one for each line.

There is a problem.

Some python packages, e.g. [jira](https://github.com/pycontribs/jira), are using a thingy called [pbr](https://pypi.org/project/pbr/) that I hadn't come across before.

When pbr starts up, it [reads](https://git.openstack.org/cgit/openstack-dev/pbr/tree/pbr/packaging.py#n775) the package's PKG-INFO or METADATA files.  Those are stored under the `<pkg>-<version>.dist-info` directory.

When this directory is missing, the whole lambda [barfs](https://git.openstack.org/cgit/openstack-dev/pbr/tree/pbr/packaging.py#n833) at startup with this error:

> module initialization error
> Versioning for this project requires either an sdist tarball, or access to an upstream git repository. It's also possible that there is a mismatch between the package name in setup.cfg and the argument given to pbr.version.VersionInfo. Project name jira was given, but was not able to be found.

Only solution I have found is to remove `*.dist-info` from the blacklist, which involves manually patching serverless-wsgi.

Maybe we should leave the `*.dist-info` directory, and just blacklist the `RECORD` file, as that's the only one which seems to change between deployments?

1. If I understand correctly, aliasing `cached_property` to `cachedproperty` implies that what is now called `cached_property` should be renamed to, say, `single_threaded_cached_property`. Is this what you have in mind?
2. I don't think that the thread-safe version should be the default for a few reasons:
   - Multithreading is not very common in Python due to the GIL.
   - Many/most Python data structures and patterns are not thread safe, e.g. the simple augmented assignment operators.
   - It is slower than the unthreaded one.

    A clear note about thread safety in the docs is just fine IMO.

Tkinter is notoriously unsafe with threading, so your script may work at low rates, but I suspect that the thread interface is interrupting the canvas draws.  When working with tkinter, use the `after`.  I have re-written your script to do the same thing with much better results:

```
import tkinter as tk
import tk_tools
import random

class Runner:
    def __init__(self, parent, rscale, pause):
        self.parent = parent
        self.rscale = rscale
        self.pause = pause

        self.parent.after(self.pause, self.run)

    def run(self):
        self.rscale.set_value(random.randint(0, 101))
        self.parent.after(self.pause, self.run)

root = tk.Tk()
rs = tk_tools.RotaryScale(root, max_value=100.0, size=100, unit='km/h')
rs.grid(row=0, column=0)

Runner(root, rs, 1)

root.mainloop()
```

According to [the libusb FAQ item on OSX usage](https://github.com/libusb/libusb/wiki/FAQ#how-can-i-run-libusb-applications-under-mac-os-x-if-there-is-already-a-kernel-extension-installed-for-the-device), it should not be a permissions issue (which was my first guess, as this is typically the first issue on linux), but could be that there is a kernel driver already managing the device. There is also an [item about HID devices](https://github.com/libusb/libusb/wiki/FAQ#does-libusb-support-usb-hid-devices), which encourages the use of HIDAPI (no idea about python a binding for it though). Although it is not clear what happens when using libusb without described setup, it seems likely the devices are not visible.

About support channel, you're at the right place to start for issues using python-libusb1. If we cannot find a python-wrapper-specific cause and the issue persists, the libusb mailing list would be the next place to report the issue indeed.

How do you try to login? I suspect a config issue.

Have a look at the docs, LoginView is explained quite detailed:
https://james1345.github.io/django-rest-knox/views/

rest-knox will only give tokens to users that are authenticated. Simply because if you have a token you no longer need to access the LoginView. And if you don't have a token, rest-knox does not know who you are. You need password and username to receive a token.

To make your user authenticated, you can e.g. use a form that creates a django session or BasicAuth where you send the credentials per post request. But don't try to send a token to knox's LoginView.

e.g. with [httpie](https://httpie.org/) `http -ja user@domain.com:secretpassword POST myhost.com/api/auth/login/`

On success, you will receive a token that you then use for any following request. 

Django provides views for authenticating via a form out of the box. See last code box under this caption:
https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/topics/auth/default/#the-login-required-decorator

Hope that clears up your misunderstanding.

OK.... have a general idea now of how to make something simpler than now....

Beginning in version 2.12 the examples and documentation will use a new term for the high-level functions, that term being "Popup".

On the output side, `MsgBox` simply gets a new name  -  `Popup`

All of the high-level functions will be renamed using the word Popup in the name.  Additionally, the return values will be changed for these from 2 return codes to a single code.  This fact alone means a new set of names to not break existing code.

On the input side, the naming convention is to still use "Popup" as the pre-fix, but adds Get____ to indicate it's an input dialog box.

```
PopupGetText
PopupGetFile
PopupGetFolder
```

This ties together the high-level APIs by having the `Popup` keyword be a part of all of the names.

The return values collapse from 2 to 1.

If the user closes the input box by clicking cancel or by clicking the "X", the return value will be None.  Note that this is different than the user entering nothing which will generate a return value of ''.

The issue is that the default `ModelAdmin.get_search_results` implementation doesn't do an exact lookup like you're doing, instead it does an `__icontains` by default. So this will probably be fixed in the same way as #10 which is to implement custom lookup handling for the field, so all of those different lookups succeed. The issue is figuring out how to handle any of the case-insensitive lookups (`iexact`, `icontains` `istartswith`, `iendswith`, etc) and also the partial lookup types themselves, since hashids are very much case-sensitive, and we don't store the strings, so do partial matches is problematic.

Apologies for the late reply, I must have overlooked this ticket. I hope my answer is still relevant.

Djangos password hashing system depends on every hash having a prefix like `bcrypt$` identifying the hash algorithm used. You cannot have hashes without such a prefix in the database. 

The converters (see relevant chapter in the README!) are not intended to be used in `PASSWORD_HASHERS` but to convert stock bcrypt hashes to hashes usable by Django and back. In case of bcrypt, nothing really happens but the adding the `bcrypt$` prefix (or stripping it, when converting back). So you can use the converter to produce usable hashes for Django and then use Djangos stock `BCryptPasswordHasher`.

@zhanghang1989

`kernel_size` must be odd number, otherwise strange things happen! It's mentioned in the docs string, but I think it would be better for this to be added as strict validation to avoid confusion like this in the future.

With regards to the stride (once a odd `kernel_size` is used), `stride1` relates to the striding on the `data1` feature map 1 and `stride2` relates to the striding within the 'neighborhood' defined on feature map 2. Without a neighborhood this method would compare all patches from feature map 1 with all patches from feature map 2. With large feature maps, the number of comparisons becomes too large, so we restrict to a region in feature map 2 centered around the corresponding location of the patch in feature map 1. And `stride2` strides within this neighborhood region.

I recommend taking a look the ['FlowNet: Learning Optical Flow with Convolutional Networks'](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1504.06852.pdf ) paper that defined this type of layer.

Well, if backwards-incompatible/breaking changes are on the table... 😉 

What'd I'd really like to do is something like what I [suggested on #1104](https://github.com/django-extensions/django-extensions/issues/1104#issuecomment-341847301). More specifically, I'd remove the `--notebook` and `--kernel` options for `shell_plus`, and add a new command, maybe just called `install_kernel`. This would behave and have options similar to [`python -m ipykernel install`](https://ipython.readthedocs.io/en/latest/install/kernel_install.html#kernels-for-different-environments)  and the [bash kernel](https://github.com/takluyver/bash_kernel) with respect to installing a kernel spec, but would add the `django_extensions` notebook extension. I'd try to have helpful defaults, like defaulting to `--user` with a name that reflects the active virtual environment.

With the kernel spec installed, users would be free to run `jupyter notebook`, `jupyter lab`, `jupyter qtconsole`, etc. in whatever environment they want (their Django virtualenv, a Jupyter virtualenv, Anaconda, etc.). And, `django_extensions` wouldn't need the `NOTEBOOK_ARGUMENTS` and `IPYTHON_ARGUMENTS` settings, because it's no longer acting as a wrapper around the `notebook` command, and users would be free to edit the installed kernel spec as needed.

It's a big change, but it seems more in line with how Jupyter kernels are designed to work. I'm happy to take ownership of this, including updating the docs. What do y'all think?

Hey @dylanlingelbach, we don't flow the api object in the manner you're describing (but that might be a good PR). The flow you're using would require the following call after you instantiate the `FacebookAdsApi` object:

```
api = FacebookAdsApi(session)
FacebookAdsApi.set_default_api(api)
```

Alternatively, you can skip creating the session and creating the `FacebookAdsApi` object by doing the following:

```
api = FacebookAdsApi.init(app_id, app_secret_access_token)
me = AdUser(fbid='me', api=api)
ad_account = me.get_ad_account()
assert ad_account.get_api() == api
```

@datakurre - I wanted to have it decoupled from the structure pattern. The title and description are outside the scope of the structure pattern and those two are only two aspects of UI elements, which should potentially be updated (whereas description is not available in standard Plone folder_contents).
In concrete projects, there might be other ones too - or someone also needs to have the section viewlet and breadcrumbs viewlet be updated. If that's the case, then you can more or less easily overwrite the RequireJS configuration of that pattern and use a own implementation.

Regarding the section and breadcrumbs viewlet - I didn't want to include their update in the pattern because:
- A costly AJAX call would be needed.
- A view-wrapper would be needed too - which would introduce a new concept (wrapping viewlets in views), where we already have a better one - tiles. But they are not yet approved for Core...

Makes sense? I do not have a very strong opinion on how I implemented that pattern...

On El Capitan, Apple stopped shipping the OpenSSL headers, so to install `sops` one needs to first install the cryptography package with the following command: `env LDFLAGS="-L$(brew --prefix openssl)/lib" CFLAGS="-I$(brew --prefix openssl)/include" pip install cryptography`.
which is significantly more complex that just running `pip install sops`, but there's no way around it.

Yes, the blocker is that the curve had not been implemented in OpenSSL until recently.  I believe the correct patch for that file will be:

```patch
--- a/pgpy/_curves.py
+++ b/pgpy/_curves.py
@@ -48,7 +48,19 @@
     key_size = 512


+@utils.register_interface(ec.EllipticCurve)
+class X25519(object):
+    name = 'X25519'
+    key_size = 256
+
+
+@utils.register_interface(ec.EllipticCurve)
+class Ed25519(object):
+    name = 'ed25519'
+    key_size = 256
+
+
 # add these curves to the _CURVE_TYPES list
-for curve in [BrainpoolP256R1, BrainpoolP384R1, BrainpoolP512R1]:
+for curve in [BrainpoolP256R1, BrainpoolP384R1, BrainpoolP512R1, X25519, Ed25519]:
     if curve.name not in ec._CURVE_TYPES and curve.name in _openssl_get_supported_curves():
         ec._CURVE_TYPES[curve.name] = curve
```

pgpy/constants.py will need to be updated to also import X25519 and Ed25519 from _curves, and add references to them to class EllipticCurveOID. That should then be enough to start actually using those curves with PGPy, provided that your install of OpenSSL is new enough to include them. I will add these changes for the next release.

So I did some experiment here (and I will clean them up soon).  The root cause is in the memory leak checker. In short, deleting a (bunch of) object does not necessarily free their memory immediately. In the sensitive situation like this, we will need a gc.collect() to avoid false positive (see that successful [experiment 1](https://github.com/rayluo/botocore/commit/fc6eae2f3ae4130dc67e1ee037033a8d2ada9d14)). I'll come up with a complete adjustment on memory leak checker in a separate PR, and then rebase this one.

we can't have `from ddtrace.contrib.asyncio import tracer` appear anywhere in user or integration code or thigns won't play well together.

what i think we need is

```
# in the configuration code
ddtrace.tracer = async.Tracer()

# in other code (being completely safe)
import ddtrace as dd

with dd.tracer.trace(...) as span:
    pass

# ... or making sure you're importing after configuration
from ddtrace import tracer
with tracer.trace(...) as span:

@sbma44 introducing users to parallel requests is going to run them up against the rate limits pretty quickly. Say we create 12 threads and each makes a batch of 50 geocode queries... there's a UX issue to work out.

The mbx-geocode command has some support for batching built in already: it can get queries from stdin and doesn't presume that there's only one. We could collect queries into batches, send them to the batch geocode endpoint, and write out results out the batch, syncronously. If we gave users an async option, maybe we would also put on them the responsibility of looking in the query object of the responses instead of trusting in any order. There are already some standard methods in Python that behave like this. The multiprocessing module's [imap_unordered()](https://docs.python.org/2/library/multiprocessing.html#multiprocessing.pool.multiprocessing.Pool.imap_unordered) is one.

You need to specify the name of your package, or the package manager won’t know how to call your package, and Pipenv won’t be able to know what you mean by the `foo` package.

The minimal fix is to modify your `setup.py`:

```python
import setuptools

setuptools.setup(
    name='foo',
    packages=setuptools.find_packages(),
)
```

Your `setup.py` (without `name`) is technically valid (technically `setup.py` can contain anything as long as it can be executed by the interpreter), but *practically* you need a few more fields to make the package actually work in the Python ecosystem.

I would prefer if we remove OpenMP completely. This does not really resolve the concerns I mentioned in #97.

If we offer the option, we'll have packagers and users using OpenMP and have to support it, and it's a portability concern for Astropy core.

Also we have to test it. Note that this PR leaves the option to use OpenMP, but as far as I can see it's completely untested in CI now.

Also: it hasn't really been shown that this multi-threading gives good speed-ups. There have been some benchmarks posted in the PRs adding OpenMP that show a factor 2 speed-up for large arrays (and slow-down for small arrays), but not that our HEALPix C lib is not efficient yet, it can be improved by a factor of a few (see e.g. https://github.com/astropy/astropy-healpix/issues/34#issuecomment-401709090 and https://github.com/astropy/astropy-healpix/issues/34#issuecomment-401791023 ) and once it's optimised it's not clear if using many cores helps a lot.

So @astrofrog - If you want to keep the OpenMP, then I agree this is an improvement and we should merge.

To my knowledge, when you combine code with separate licenses and mix a bit (which is what's going to happen in a port), technically _the most restrictive license wins_ in the resulting combination. Both of these licenses are highly permissive but they are not equivalent. The modified BSD is more restrictive than the MIT (requires posting of the license file, etc.). That would mean `colorspacious`, by accepting our ported code, would have to relicense under the 3-clause BSD - unless we authorize a port of our code under the MIT license.

I do not know the process for taking code touched by a _lot_ of hands over the years and authorizing it under a different license. While I don't think many would disagree, to my knowledge that is a barrier here. 

I'm not a lawyer, but I did a fair amount of research into this.

I tried out a version using `dask.threaded` as well where the parallel section is as:

```python
    avg_y = 0
    if num_workers == 1:
        # serial processing
        for shift in all_shifts:
            avg_y += _run_one_shift(shift)
    else:
        # dask-based parallel
        for shift in all_shifts:
            avg_y += dask.delayed(_run_one_shift)(shift)
        avg_y = avg_y.compute(get=dask.threaded.get, num_workers=num_workers)

    return avg_y / nshifts
```

Performance was nearly identical to the `futures`-based implementation.  I guess the main benefit of `dask`  here is that it could be easily adapted to distributed computation if desired.

@Britefury thanks for submitting this! I hadn't heard of SEEDS and it looks to me like a great algorithm, worthy of inclusion in scikit-image. 

That's the good news. The bad news is that I think the implementation will need a lot of refinement before getting merged. Functions like `refine_blocks`, `refine_pixels`, and `seeds` are huge monoliths that will be very difficult to maintain in the future.

A lot of the code can be simplified using scipy and numpy functions. In fact, from what I've seen, the implementation can be made n-dimensional *and* the code simplified, to boot. For example, instead of your 2D histogram downsampling function, you can use `numpy.add.reduceat` to downsample a histogram of any dimension:

```python
import numpy as np
# 2D histogram for 9 x 16 image, 3 bins
h = np.random.rand(9, 16, 3)
hs = h
for ax in range(hs.ndim - 1):
    hs = np.add.reduceat(hs, np.arange(0, hs.shape[ax] - 1, 2), axis=ax)
```

`hs` will hold the downsampled histogram.

@scottpurdy Yeah, almost. After a few quick tests I noted 3 things that differ from my "reserved slots" approach.

1 - Performance: The SDRCategoryEncoder uses random to assign categories. This overhead doesn't exists on the original Category Encoder.
2 - Sparseness: The SDRCategoryEncoder provides a more sparse representation. I don't know if that's actually useful, as one generally passes the output of the Encoder to the SpatialPooler anyway.
3 - Handling of Slots Run Out: The SDRCategoryEncoder gave me a RuntimeError when it couldn't find a unique pattern to represent a novel input. In the original CategoryEncoder, as in my approach, it just returns the representation of the category <UNKOWN>. Which one is more useful depends on the use case, I guess.

No, the TM doesn't store the active columns, since it receives them as input, so the caller already knows the active columns. It doesn't offer a `cellsToColumns` method because:

1. Callers can implement it just as easily -- just divide the cells by `tm.getCellsPerColumn()`. Even Python callers can do this with numpy arrays.
2. Different callers have different expectations. Some callers would prefer a method that translates a list of cells into a list of columns, maintaining indices (such that `columns[42]` is the column for `cells[42]`), while others would want duplicates removed (like the method above).

Here is promised summary of my comments, most of which we discussed
IRL on Monday.

**WRT** use cases, there are some more, e.g. the linking in Periodicals or
among Institutes, e.g. several other kinds of inter-record linking in
CERN Open Data portal, such as "constituent unit entry", tag 774.  See
[open data JSON MARC configuration](https://github.com/cernopendata/opendata.cern.ch/blob/master/invenio_opendata/base/recordext/fields/opendata.cfg).

**WRT** proposed table, we may need an extra column storing properties of
the relation.  Say record R1 is a photo that was used as B/W on page 7
of record R2, and as RGB on page 2 of record R3.  Where to store this
information?  It logically belongs with the relation, not with the
records themselves.  Hence perhaps a new PostgreSQL JSON column
representing abstract key-value properties store, where people can
store anything they need to describe the properties of the
relationship.  (Kind of like "more info" store in invenio/master.)

(**WRT** various types of relationships, looking at all the various
subfields existing in the MARC relational tags shows that this
property store can become rather big.  Hence perhaps a question
whether an abstract column would be enough or whether we need some
further structure?  I guess a generic JSON column might be enough,
since [it is usually fast](http://simko.home.cern.ch/simko/postgresql-mongodb-json-select-speed.html) to search there, however the plethora of 
relationship properties may require some deeper thoughts.)

**WRT** records vs fields as first-class linkable citizens, wouldn't it be
nice to use UUIDs for almost everything, and allow linking on any part
of JSON nested level.  (See @aw-bib's example of inter-field linking
inside the same MARC record.)  Kind of decorate parts of JSON in a LD
style, meaning "factor this tree out for me into a new record in
another JSON store and link them together".  This would be great for
low-level system users.

(If we make also fields linkable, then this mechanism could be used
for poor-man's authority record control, e.g. field representing
"arXiv subject category" has only ~10 values (hep-th, hep-ph, etc) and
so could just as well live as a link to this "JSON based knowledge
base", so to speak.  When do we use "by-reference linking" and where
do we stop and copy values and use "multi-edit" to manage the linking
information?)

**WRT** possibly representing relation information in record's "master
format" data, we have several use cases: (1) developers or system
librarians are working with JSON all the way as much as possible; (2)
cataloguers and curators might use tools the developers write, or
might prefer to work with "native aster format" such as MARC or
UNIMARC or EAD that is in use on the given concrete Invenio
installation; (3) general public will be served any kind of
"serialised version" of the record, in read-only manner, so this is
less critical.  My concern is about the second category, where we may
need to offer read-write like access to non-JSON formats.

E.g. imagine a site receiving MARC authority records from a third
party; if they want to enrich the information, and send it back, and
if they keep receiving and sending updates to these records, both from
third party and from authors, say, then do we want to represent the
relationship information only in the JSON or also in this "editable
native format" that cataloguers are working with?  If we speak about
an Invenio installation that runs with maybe half an IT person and
five cataloguer persons, using some MS Windows EAD editor or
something, and constantly receiving MARC authority records from the
National Library, the answer would be yes.  Hence, if we represent
linked information in these "native editable formats", we should make
things really lossless, which can go wild.  Would cataloguers agree to
see and edit UUIDs?  Would we hide them semi-transparently somehow?

IOW, would we aim at lossless representation of the relationship
information also in the "native editable format" the concrete site may
use (or not), which can get quite complex just looking at generating
MARC out of the relationship property store?  And if we do, would we
want to represent it in a nice editable manner, not exposing internal
UUIDs too much?

**WRT** using a graph database, this should be definitely seriously
considered...

>  in practice the rejection sampling near the origin ends up in an infinite loop waiting for a deviate smaller than 1/inf. Any suggestions?

Where exactly is the problem happening?  Making the `OneDimensionalDeviate`?  Or when drawing from the interval (`Interval::drawWithin`)?  

Anyway, some things you could try:
- Don't go all the way to 0 for the range you provide to the `OneDimensionalDeviate` constructor.  i.e. range[0] = epsilon.  You can figure out what epsilon is appropriate based on `shoot_accuracy`, which dictates how much flux you are allowed to miss when shooting.
- Similarly, you can have the radial function that you give to `OneDimensionalDeviate` flatten off at the origin in a way that misses less than `shoot_accuracy` worth of flux.  In this case, you would want to have range be [ 0, epsilon, shoot_maxr ], since you'll want to let it know that the profile changes non-smoothly at whatever epsilon where you flatten it out.
- Or rather than miss any flux, you can have the central region at r < epsilon have a higher surface brightness such that the total flux there matches what the real profile would have.  Just the s.b. would be discontinuous at r=epsilon (so definitely need to include that value in the `range`).  The appropriate value for epsilon then would be where 2pi int_0^epsilon( r abs(f_true(r) - f_approx(r)) ) < shoot_accuracy.  i.e. the integrated absolute error in the flux.
- You could try to get even more fancy by trying to come up with a function to use at r < epsilon that has the right flux and the right value at r=epsilon, but has f(0) < infinity (and presumably would have a discontinuous first derivative at r=epsilon).  I'm not sure that this is worth it, but you could think about it.

> I noticed a couple weeks ago that the Spergel profile is the F.T. of the Moffat profile. Not sure how to take advantage of that but I thought it was interesting. :)

Aside from using the analytic form in `kValue`, probably not much else to do.  The Moffat class already uses this in reverse.  We used to use a tabulated FT, before I discovered the analytic version -- it was a significant speed up to switch to using `cyl_bessel_k`.

I am nearly done putting together a sample of I<25.2 galaxies in format analogous to the 23.5 sample.  The only thing left to be finalized is the file to be used to impose selection criteria (masking etc.).

So I need to make sure the code can work with the new tarball just as well as with the old one.  Here is my thinking:

- Currently the catalog file has a particular name, and the parametric fits file can be constructed from it by taking ```blah.fits``` (catalog name) and turning it into ```blah_fits.fits```.  So that's fine.

- The files with images can be named whatever, because ```blah.fits``` specifies the filenames.

Currently, for the 23.5 sample, ```blah=real_galaxy_catalog_23.5``` and for the new one, ```blah=real_galaxy_catalog_25.2```.  The files with images also use the same naming scheme, replacing 23.5 with 25.2.

- The bit that could be confusing is that for the current tarball, the selection file is called ```real_galaxy_galsim_selection.fits```.  It doesn't say 23.5 in it.  In retrospect, I think I should have used a name scheme like ```blah_selection.fits``` so that the filename can be constructed from the catalog file. - Other files in the tarball are the README and the noise file. We still need the functionality of cloning from a specific member or set of members, i.e. those that is located in the same region/availability zone/datacenter as the replica to create. The former clonefrom could implement it, because you could set the node to clone from explicitly.

What we can do here is to agree on a certain tag so that all nodes having this tag are considered close enough to be the clone destination for each other (iff they have also the clonefrom tag set), and provide a new callback that calls a script during startup that will gather and set arbitrary tags on the node. For instance, on AWS the callback can detect the current region and set it as a value of the tag above. This way, we can provide a vendor-independant mechanism to choose the preferred replicas to clone (and also to replicate) from.

It make sense to resolve http://localhost:2379 into http://127.0.0.1:2379, but for https urls must stay unchanged.

@joguSD Has this feature been implemented yet? I not, I would like to work on it. Do you have any pointers, please?

> I do think it would be nice to have an abstraction to handle partial failures so you can make sure you have seen all of your messages at least once

No, that's not the issue I'm getting at.  The default behavior now (the comes for free from lambda) is that they either all succeed if your lambda function returns successfully, or the *all* fail.  This means that out of a batch of 10, if I have 9 that I successfully handle, and 1 that fails (and raises an exception to indicate the failure), then I will see *all 10 message again*.  There's never a case where you won't see your messages at least once.

The behavior that I want is that if 9 messages succeed and 1 fails, then I should *only* see the 1 failed message again.  The other 9 are deleted.

> Seems a little too implicit as we are not polling for messages. 

Can you elaborate on how this is too implicit, my suggestion was to let the user tell us somehow which succeeded and which didn't ("It seems like we should provide some way for a user to say that an individual message was successfully handled.")  If a user tells us a message was handled successfully, it seems reasonable they would expect not to see the message again.

The thing that I'm not a fan of (and that we can't change) is that an exception must be raised to tell lambda not to delete any messages.  I would prefer if we had a way where we didn't have to use exceptions but could let lambda know which ones succeeded and which ones failed.  Essentially I want uncaught exceptions to be reserved for cases where things really went wrong.  I'd like something more like dynamodb's batch write items (i.e a list of items that succeeded and a list of items that failed).

Let me know if I'm still not making sense and I can try to put together a more concrete example.

Hi, I answered this question in a stack overflow post [here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/49319575/aws-x-ray-shows-duplicate-nodes-in-the-service-map/49660589#49660589).

But I copy/paste my answer here regardless:

The node you see comes from this PR https://github.com/aws/aws-xray-sdk-python/pull/19 for adding support to httplib. If you use `patch_all` httplib operations will also be captured. In your case the dependency tree is `boto3` -> `botocore` -> vendored `requests` -> `httplib`. So your AWS subsegment will have children subsegments that representing operations executed by `httplib`.

The service graph however will render a "remote" subsegment as a node which is what you see. The recommended way is to explicit patch the library you want to capture to avoid unexpected behavior.

```def list_gcs_directories(bucket, prefix):
    # from https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/issues/920

    prefixes = set()
    for page in iterator.pages:

Pools from `multiprocess` are *exactly* what you get from `multiprocessing`, but with the `pickle` serializer replaced with `dill`.  That's it.

`pathos` provides additional workflow, like preserving pools, and pools for sending objects across the network.

Is it possible to have hierarchical pools with `multiprocess` -- yes.  Should you put a `ProcessPool` inside a `ProcessPool` -- no.  It's not good to nest the same type of pool; different types of pools are fine, just not the same type.

```console
$ ./stacker.yaml.py | stacker build -

Where `stacker.yaml.py` is a little python script that generates our stacker config file. There's really nothing you _can't_ do with stacker using this approach, since you have the full power of the unix pipeline at your disposal.

I ended up creating a wrapper class for `Template` that lets me define the group and label at the time the parameter is added to the template. This way I don't have to worry about keeping the two in sync. Adding below in case this is useful to anyone else and/or could be incorporated into Troposphere proper some day.

    from collections import OrderedDict

    from troposphere import Template


    class InterfaceTemplate(Template):
        """
        Custom Template class that allows us to optionally define groups and labels for
        CloudFormation Parameters at the time they're added to the template. Groups and
        labels specified, if any, will be added to a custom AWS::CloudFormation::Interface
        """

        def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
            super(InterfaceTemplate, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)
            # use OrderedDict() so we can keep track of the order in which groups are added
            self.parameter_groups = OrderedDict()
            self.parameter_labels = {}
            self.group_order = []

        def add_parameter(self, parameter, group=None, label=None):
            """
            Save group and/or label, if specified, for later generation of
            'AWS::CloudFormation::Interface' in to_dict().
            """
            parameter = super(InterfaceTemplate, self).add_parameter(parameter)
            if group:
                if group not in self.parameter_groups:
                    self.parameter_groups[group] = []
                self.parameter_groups[group].append(parameter.title)
            if label:
                self.parameter_labels[parameter.title] = label
            return parameter

        def set_group_order(self, group_order):
            """
            Set an ordered list of all known, possible parameter groups in this stack.
            If none is provided, groups will appear in the order they were first passed
            to add_parameter().
            """
            self.group_order = group_order

        def to_dict(self):
            """
            Overwrite 'AWS::CloudFormation::Interface' key in self.metadata (if any)
            with the groups and labels defined via add_parameter(), and then call
            super().to_dict().
            """
            # create an ordered list of parameter groups for our interface
            ordered_groups = list(self.group_order)
            groups_in_stack = list(self.parameter_groups.keys())
            # add any groups specified in the stack that we didn't know about in advance
            ordered_groups += [g for g in groups_in_stack if g not in ordered_groups]
            # remove any groups NOT specified in the stack
            ordered_groups = [g for g in ordered_groups if g in groups_in_stack]
            # update metadata with our interface
            self.metadata.update({
                'AWS::CloudFormation::Interface': {
                    'ParameterGroups': [
                        {
                            'Label': {'default': group},
                            'Parameters': self.parameter_groups[group],
                        }
                        for group in ordered_groups
                    ],
                    'ParameterLabels': dict([
                        (parameter, {'default': label})
                        for parameter, label in self.parameter_labels.items()
                    ]),
                }
            })
            return super(InterfaceTemplate, self).to_dict()

This is an issue with DistributionNotFound from pkg_resources providing wonky exception arguments.  e.args[0] is the actual Requirement and e.args[1] is the set of project_names of the last hop of projects requiring e.args[0].  This means that deleting e.args[0] from the reqs will not be sufficient to prevent an infinite loop here.  I think the only way around this is to resolve one req at a time.

1.) Yes, this is a NPM concept: https://docs.npmjs.com/misc/scope
2.) Yes, that's correct. NPM/Yarn are CLIs come with a lot of features that would take a large effort to support in Pants.
3.) During resolution phase is in reference to the "Pants `resolve` task execution". We define a "resolve.node" task that is able to download thirdparty dependencies using the NPM/Yarn package manager as well as other node_packages like node_remote_module, node_preinstalled_module, etc... The task uses NPM/Yarn under the hood and URLs are resolved via the lockfile,shrinkwrap,etc..
4.) There are two places we'd like to install packages and sources to. One is under the `.pants.d` which we already do. The other will be directly on the source target roots directly. Npm resolves the dependencies relative to the package.json in the node_modules directory by default and also serves as  a local cache. The former installation is used for running goals like testing and the latter for setting up a development environment.

I totally agree that we should distinguish bucketing from data cleaning. However I think it's totally fine if Sockeye decides to not use 100% of the training data in order to make the model fit into memory. Also I'd rather not have any buckets on either the source or the target side that exceed max_seq_len.

I agree that we should reduce the bias we introduce by these mechanisms. Already by sorting the data and then fitting them into batches we introduce quite some bias. For the particular example you gave you could still come up with sentences of length ratio 0.75 that would fit in the last bucket, such as (91, 68). So the last bucket would not just contain data with length ratio 0.7 or smaller. But you're right that for the sentences going towards the max source length we limit ourselves to sentences with a length_ratio smaller than the average. 

Anyway, having a deviation from the length_ratio like you suggested seems like a good compromise between on memory usage of the (max_seq_len, max_seq_len) solution and what we currently have. For example we could create an additional bucket with (max_seq_len, length_ratio * max_seq_len + window_size) to keep things simple.

This looks really interesting. Thanks a lot for bringing it here. I don't think we ever did manage to figure out how to add metadata to generic uri's when we add them to the queue.

A few comments. SoCo would like to be a "highlevel" API, so hopefully the user shouldn't need to know anything about the underlying technologies. Therefore, I think it would be better if it was also possible to use this, without constructing the metadata by hand. So if you have generic metadata template that works for any uri, then I would suggest adding an extra optional argument for the title and if this is given use that to construct the metadata inside the method.

On the more technical side:

* It does not look as if the default empty string for the metadata is actually used as an empty string. Therefore I think it is cleaner to make the default argument None and check for that. I know it is done in this way other places in the library, but no reason to continue ;)

There are few more technical details I'll add as inline comments.

But once again, this looks really great, can't wait to get it in.

I think what @bcsaller is getting at is that if we're running into conflicts in config options that aren't correctly resolved by accepting the higher layer's version, then it might mean the option isn't worthwhile to expose to the end user (Juju admin).

In particular, for this example, if the webapp is being proxied behind nginx, then it doesn't really make sense to expose the port it's listening on as a config option, as that value is only useful to facilitate communication between nginx and the webapp.  Instead, the charm or app should just choose a high, unused port (or have a default that is unlikely to conflict) and send that to nginx.

There's quite a few oddities with logging in Components ( and ApplicationRunner) stemming from txaio:
https://github.com/crossbario/txaio/issues/75
https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/issues/674#issuecomment-225867970

The general advice for configuring logging in libraries is don't mess too much, just emit log events and let the app developer do their own logging configuration:
https://docs.python.org/3/howto/logging.html#configuring-logging-for-a-library
http://docs.python-guide.org/en/latest/writing/logging/#logging-in-a-library

Currently AB/txaio is messing too much with how stdlib logging works due to the choices made in txaio:

1.  `start_logging()` function attaches a handler to the root logger with default formatting and logging to stdout making app level logging configuration annoying e.g duplicate log events if you attach another handler to the root logger

2. in aio.py `make_logger()` always returns the root logger so all AB aio log events log to this object instead of an 'autobahn.*' namespace

3. info is the default loglevel even when not using `start_logging()` so `logging.debug()` is made a noop preventing `logger.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)` from working at app level.  This is contrary to what's stated in the last line of this section: https://autobahn.readthedocs.io/en/latest/wamp/programming.html#logging

I'll submit a couple of PRs to txaio repo to address 2 and 3.

Point 1 only applies if using `run([comp1, comp2], log_level='info'`) as it currently calls `start_logging()` whereas `comp1.start()` way of starting a component does not, but I think `run()` should be modified to remove the `log_level` argument and not use `start_logging()` either.  Yes it's a convenience method of running components but the AB lib shouldn't be starting logging too, that's an app level thing configured by the user via stdlib logging or tx's version.  

If run() is modified and the txaio PRs go in there would be no need to configure loglevels at the component level, both run() and start() users could then configure logging in the app like this:

```
logging.basicConfig(format=FORMAT, stream=sys.stdout)
myapp_logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
myapp_logger.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)

ab_logger = logging.getLogger('autobahn')
ab_logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)
```
allowing independent logging levels of the app and AB.

Downside is no noop optimization of logleve calls in AB - how big of a performance issue is this?
Maybe we could create a module function like `optimize_logging(log_level=lvl)` which calls `txaio.set_global_log_level(lvl)` under the hood if needed.

If you look at the decode method, the procedure to extract this information goes something like:
- [If the predicted antecedent is -1 (the null class), we don't do anything.](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/master/allennlp/models/coreference_resolution/coref.py#L334)
- If there is a prediction for this particular span, it is _with respect to the other spans which survived pruning_, so we need to convert a relative span index to an index into `top_spans` from which we can extract the actual text span.
- Update a cluster if the span is not already part of the cluster.

So overall - `antecedent_indices[i]` does not hold any meaning unless `predicted_antecedents[i] != -1`, in which case predicted_antecedent_span_for_span_j = top_spans[antecedent_indices[predicted_antecedents[j]]]. Sorry for the confusion - if you submit a PR to make all irrelevant indices in `decode` -1, that would probably be a good idea for readability.

The requirement that spans are co-referent with things before it is an implementation detail, as this model considers co-reference as a clustering problem only. In your example these would simply be clustered together.

The way you have to set it up is special case and a path that isn't available to general users.  I'm only doing it for one thing I couldn't 12-factorize without significant effort.  I'll happily migrate to a supported path once it exists.

You can't deploy a good majority of service Dockerfiles because they require Docker volumes for persistence.  Buildpacks are fine because they generally are apps you've rolled yourself with S3 support or built for Heroku with intrinsic 12-factorization.  The HA present in the deis cluster is nice, but the app gets overwritten on config:set, deploy, limits:set, external forces, etc.  Deis realized this quickly and ditched(ish) container volumes and instead created the Ceph cluster to hold its end of the bargain (DB backups with wal-e, log storage via CephFS, etc.).  #231 is needed, but getting those services to survive in a HA way is half the battle and one not easily accomplished.

A Ceph Service Broker with a pathway to get that filespace into Docker containers would do well towards that. Deis has crafted an innovative, functional solution that should be reusable in the service layer.

>Yes, py35 compat is required

Understand. Do you mind to elaborate on the reasoning? Not trying to argue against it. I just want to learn.

>I would prefer we annotate variables

Here's how one would usually approach this in TypeScript. It's quite a fair comparison because TypeScript also adds type annotations to a dynamic language (JS) and has similar features as `mypy` such as type inference.

I wouldn't add type annotations in cases like this:

```
foo = 'str'
```
Due to the type inference, `mypy` is smart enough to disallow this.

```
foo = 'str'
foo = 5
```
Raises an error as expected
```
$ mypy test.py 
test.py:16: error: Incompatible types in assignment (expression has type "int", variable has type "str")
```
So, adding type annotations doesn't add any safety in this case. I'd consider it rather noisy tbh.

Especially when you keep in mind that we have to use Py35 compatible comment annotations. Comment type annotations are rather fragile compared to proper ones.

Consider this:

```
foo = 4 # ttype: str
```

`mypy` checks this without errors even though we obviously intended this to be of type `str`. It also doesn't catch that we mistyped anything. It will simply see this as an `int`.

Let's take a look at another case and imagine we have a class with an instance variable that is initially set to `None`. This case is more tricky as `None` doesn't let `mypy` infer the type.

However, `mypy` can still infer the type based on the context.

```
class Foo ():
  expected_to_be_int = None

  def blows_up(self) -> float:
    if self.expected_to_be_int is not None:
      return self.expected_to_be_int / 2
    
    return 0

val = Foo()
val.expected_to_be_int = 'bar'
```

This blows up as expected

```
$ mypy test.py 
test.py:11: error: Incompatible types in assignment (expression has type "str", variable has type "None")
```

Also `mypy` will tell us when it needs our help because it can't infer the type.

```
val = []
```

```
$ mypy test.py 
test.py:1: error: Need type annotation for variable
```

With all that in mind, I recommend to only add the comment type annotations when `mypy` explicitly asks for them. 

If you agree with that, this PR could probably get merged. If you insist on adding comment type annotations for all variables I'll update the PR. Your call :)
