This is conda 3.18.0. If I understand this correctly, then --no-deps works in two different ways:

- When the specs are all .tar.bz2 files, then this older version of conda would install them all, determine their their dependencies, and install those. If --no-deps is supplied, it would skip this last step. The problem I'm seeing here, as it relates to conda 4.x: what if the tar files are installed, and then conda discovers that it *can't* determine a set of dependencies that will make everything work? It has _already installed_ the tar files at this point, so it can't go back.

- For normal sets of specs, --no-deps performs the full solve, including the dependencies, and _then_ drops the dependencies after the fact. I'm pretty sure we still do that, but because conda 4.x does a better solve, it's likely producing undesirable results for people.

My feeling here is that --no-deps is fundamentally problematic, and we need to find a way to address some specific uses cases and let the rest of them go. So for instance, if you have a list of .tar.bz2 files, we should certainly allow you to create an environment that contains just them, with no dependencies.
It would still be a good idea to explicitly specify the export format when calling pip. That will get rid of the warning, and you'll never have to worry about the changing default in the future.

Looking at this from a conda *usage* perspective, I would say that it is _not_ good practice to obsess over build numbers. _Version numbers_ matter, yes, because they communicate non-trivial functional differences. But build numbers, as their name suggests, ought to reflect much more minor build adjustments.

In fact, I really do believe that `channel_priority: false` is _not_ good practice. That sets you up for a game of conda update "ping-pong" when two channel providers release package upgrades on a staggered schedule. How do you know the maintainer of channel A is doing as good a job with his builds as the maintainer of channel B.

The better practice in my view is to decide which channel provider you trust, and stick with them. It will be easier to do that if we can help users make exceptions when necessary.

I've updated the changes from #64

The reason why not all compounds where loaded was the following:
**genmod** is the program that calculates all the compounds, in this case the VEP annotation was used to check if variants are in the same gene and in that case potential compounds.
VEP calls variants 5000 bases before and after a gene for `upstream_variant` and `downstream_variant` so **genmod** counts these variants as belonging to the gene.
Scout on the other hand use the gene coordinates from HGNC when uploading all variants from a gene so the upstream and downstream variants was not added.
This is now corrected so that we will load all upstream and downstream variants as well.

When we upgrade Scout to 3.3 we will reload the variants in this region for you.

The issue is that you are using non-ascii bytestrings in python 2 while having your default encoding set to ascii. When then python tries to serialize that data, it fails because the configuration doesn't match the data.

The proper fix is to use `unicode` type instead of `str` or set the default encoding properly to (I assume) `utf-8`.

Hope this helps

Hi,

thanks for the issue. Calling `clear_scroll` is not required since the scroll will be cleaned automatically once all documents are retrievede at the end of the reindex. So if everything goes well the scroll will be cleared by elasticsearch as well.

It is very hard to say what causes the slowness in your case, note that the reindex API actually has to retrieve all the document over the network into your python process and then send them back into elasticsearch. All in single thread which can slow things down considerably.

Hope this helps,

Presto provides 3 SQL statements to control transactions:
- [`START TRANSACTION`](https://prestodb.io/docs/current/sql/start-transaction.html)
- [`COMMIT`](https://prestodb.io/docs/current/sql/commit.html)
- [`ROLLBACK`](https://prestodb.io/docs/current/sql/rollback.html)

The *client* should begin the transaction by sending a `START TRANSACTION` statement with a `X-Transaction-Id=NONE` header to the *coordinator*. The *coordinator* will then set the value of `X-Transaction-Id` to the actual transaction ID to use.

The *client* will use this transaction ID for subsequent SQL statement it executes, until it either `COMMIT` or `ROLLBACK` the transaction.

[`@minrk` commented](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/issues/805#issuecomment-3772453): Right now, Cell means two things:

A. atom of execution
B. editable area

And @fperez' proposal is to have some situation where one A-Cell refers to multiple B-Cells.  Another phrasing of the same thing is essentially to have rich text comments without having to split a code block into separate executions.

With the implicit cell groups given by headings, we can actually make this work quite easily with optional behavior in 'run group', where it is _not_ run as separate completely independent executions, but concatenated prior to execution.  This is not difficult, but would lack the enforcement of a special notion of multi-textarea cell, and running the cells individually would be permitted, if not necessarily valid.

Looking at some of the issues open on send2trash (https://github.com/hsoft/send2trash/issues/9, https://github.com/hsoft/send2trash/issues/2), and thinking a bit about the complexity of the operation in general, I suspect that this would be an ongoing source of problems which we have little ability to fix. I'm not sure whether that outweighs the benefits.

One option would be to define our own trash mechanism separate from the system recycle bin. But I suspect that there's a bunch of complexity we haven't thought about, with things like network filesystems, folders shared with VMs/containers, and folders synced by things like Dropbox. It would also be annoying for people who actually want to delete things if those things were neither deleted nor placed in the normal trash.

Okay, I managed to solve it. Your advice was key, @tty02-fl; thank you very much.

I examined the -L parameter of g++.bat (that executes during `pip install fann2`) based on [this](http://ru.stackoverflow.com/a/129653) Stackoverflow answer. Its value was `C:\Users\Rencoder\Anaconda\libs`, which is where it tried (and failed) to find the _doublefann_ object file library. Should it also have searched the folders under path variable automatically?

Anyways, I manually copied the _doublefann_ object file library from my Visual Studio Release build to the Anaconda libs folder. I also had to rename the file to _doublefann_ as the Visual Studio build outputted it as _fanndouble_. This time the installer mentioned something about _doublefann_ file being incompatible. So I rebuilt the Visual Studio project with x64 solution settings instead of the default Win32 settings and replaced the object file library with the new x64 one.

`pip install` now succeeded. Thanks again, @FutureLinkOpenSource and @tty02-fl for your quick response and help with this.

> Won't that create 10 idle Spark nodes while alignment is running? @almussel pushed the scaling down to avoid wasting money on idle nodes.

Nyet! To highlight the relevant sections of the code, we create scale up ([allocate preprocessing nodes](https://github.com/fnothaft/toil-scripts/commit/4b8cdb7607aaf70883a25b5b077092b6d3add133#diff-924a4449d5efb0928b3441ac334f0c85R378)) and scale down ([post-ADAM](https://github.com/fnothaft/toil-scripts/commit/4b8cdb7607aaf70883a25b5b077092b6d3add133#diff-924a4449d5efb0928b3441ac334f0c85R390)) jobs. We then conditionally (e.g., for post-alignment, see [here](https://github.com/fnothaft/toil-scripts/commit/4b8cdb7607aaf70883a25b5b077092b6d3add133#diff-924a4449d5efb0928b3441ac334f0c85R387) and [here](https://github.com/fnothaft/toil-scripts/commit/4b8cdb7607aaf70883a25b5b077092b6d3add133#diff-924a4449d5efb0928b3441ac334f0c85R414) and [here](https://github.com/fnothaft/toil-scripts/commit/4b8cdb7607aaf70883a25b5b077092b6d3add133#diff-924a4449d5efb0928b3441ac334f0c85R438)) weave them into our DAG. This way, we don't have to touch any of the pipelines, nor do we need to keep machines turned on unnecessarily.

Also, I added code to do a scale-up during the [sample loop](https://github.com/fnothaft/toil-scripts/commit/4b8cdb7607aaf70883a25b5b077092b6d3add133#diff-924a4449d5efb0928b3441ac334f0c85R247). This was missing from the original code, but I think is necessary to make autoscaling work. Specifically, if you start with one node and wait until BWA is running to increment the cluster size, BWA schedules on a full machine (by requesting all the cores), so you'll only ever get one sample through at a time. If you bump the cluster size _before_ scheduling any of the per-sample compute, you won't block like this.

Also, I just realized a possible issue with the core count requests... @hannes-ucsc you'd mentioned this a while back, but we're requesting the full core count of a machine by using the `multiprocessing` library to query the core count on the current machine... Since we're running these clusters with a small toil-leader, I'm not sure how that'll play out. Although, I think that all of the invocations of the `multiprocessing` library occur inside of toil `Job`s and thus should be invoked on a `toil-worker`. TBD; but I think we're fine. I'm just musing out loud.

Understood.  I was admittedly conflicted when designing package's API structure.

I decided to name all of the properties and methods using the same naming convention that the Cisco Spark documentation and parallel JavaScript SDKs use.  Which, when requesting a 'list' of items (be that rooms, messages, memberships, etc.), the Spark docks and SDKs use the term 'list'.

Due to calling the method 'list', one could certainly expect a Python list to be returned; however, a Python list would have to be retrieved all at once (to be an accurate / correct / complete response to the request).  This would require retrieving all results, requesting multiple pages of responses as needed, which ultimately might not be needed if the message, room, membership or etc. that you were looking for is in the first _n_ returned items.  Generators are much more efficient for this purpose, and save the package user a lot of time and headache, and if you actually really do want a Python list, you can easily create one from any generator using the list constructor `list(generator)`.

Still to your point... The method is called 'list' (to be consistent with the docs and SDKs) and returns a generator (which might not be expected and could cause some people grief).

I have considered a few enhancements to minimize any coding issues this terms issue might cause, I could enhance the `GeneratorContainer` class to provide the following list-like capabilities (which might help close the functionality gap in use):

* Add slicing support
* Add support for common methods like `len()`
* ...others

This would make the generator containers behave more like Python lists; however, there are notable (often positive but sometimes negative) outcomes...  Like:

* Everytime you call one of those `len()` like functions... A full list must be produced - imagine running this on messages for a long-standing highly-active Spark room.
* The data is always live!  When iterating the contents, you are always seeing the latest data from the cloud (normally a great thing)!  Except when you aren't expecting the contents of list to change, like indexing an item in the list (it was at index `2` and now it is at index `5`).
* If you really do want to iterate through the same static (and potentially stale) list of items... A list really is better.  ...and with the current design, you can always create one:  `list(generator)`

No... that is code inside make_vhost_ssl.  Which is called when an ssl vhost from sites-available (not enabled) can't be located.

This creates a dummy ssl vhost file within "sites-available", but it does not enable it.  It does not put it into the configuration and the webserver is not affected.

Before it is enabled/deployed/used for anything, the cert and key directives are replaced.
Here https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/blob/master/letsencrypt_apache/configurator.py#L159

Once the directives have been changed, the vhost is deployed (enabled) here.
https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/blob/master/letsencrypt_apache/configurator.py#L217

Apache configurations often start with a "default-ssl" available host which contains the snake-oil parameters. This isn't a problem because the site isn't enabled or loaded into the web server.

The code that is currently there applies the same logic.

In general you are correct that `letsencrypt` must run on the machine pointed to by your DNS records. The benefit of doing this is that you can set up automatic renewal so you don't have to worry about your certificates expiring.

If you can/do not want to do this, you can use the [manual](https://letsencrypt.readthedocs.org/en/latest/using.html#manual) plugin to obtain a certificate while running `letsencrypt` on your local machine. With that said, this plugin lacks some polish and is still under development.

All user need 3072+ key **by default**.

People really knowing what they do can eventually reduce the size, but **by default**, Let’s encrypt must provide state of the art compliant configuration.
Standard user (99% in fact) don’t even look into the config or care about the size of the generated key or even know what TLS really is…

You're assuming that
```
ECDHE-RSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256:ECDHE-RSA-AES128-SHA256:ECDHE-RSA-AES128-SHA
```
makes more sense to administrators than
```
ECDHE-RSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256:ECDHE-ECDSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256:ECDHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384:ECDHE-ECDSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384:DHE-RSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256:DHE-DSS-AES128-GCM-SHA256:kEDH+AESGCM:ECDHE-RSA-AES128-SHA256:ECDHE-ECDSA-AES128-SHA256:ECDHE-RSA-AES128-SHA:ECDHE-ECDSA-AES128-SHA:ECDHE-RSA-AES256-SHA384:ECDHE-ECDSA-AES256-SHA384:ECDHE-RSA-AES256-SHA:ECDHE-ECDSA-AES256-SHA:DHE-RSA-AES128-SHA256:DHE-RSA-AES128-SHA:DHE-DSS-AES128-SHA256:DHE-RSA-AES256-SHA256:DHE-DSS-AES256-SHA:DHE-RSA-AES256-SHA:!aNULL:!eNULL:!EXPORT:!DES:!RC4:!3DES:!MD5:!PSK
```

While I agree the first one is neater, pretty, and saves a few bytes, my experience helping administrators is that they don't really understand the ciphersuite, regardless of its length. But they will get very frustrated if the ciphersuite breaks backward compatibility and their management comes breathing down their necks. Which is why we use a longer, uglier, but more backward compatible configuration that people will copy/paste anyway.

1. On your first machine, find the folder where your cert (or symlinks to it) are stored. This should be in `/etc/letsencrypt/live/<domain>` by default.
2. Copy all files in this folder to your 2nd machine. There should be 4 files: `cert.pem`, `privkey.pem`, `chain.pem`, and `fullchain.pem`.
3. On the 2nd machine run `letsencrypt-auto install --apache --cert-path <cert_path> --key-path <key_path> --chain-path <chain_path> --fullchain-path <fullchain_path>` where the four paths are the paths to the four files you copied over in step 2.

Preliminary conclusion: if you script the let's encrypt client, you shouldn't assume it will never fail for silly and transient reasons.  That might be true if the CA servers are down, you hit a rate limit, if this happens, or for reasons we haven't yet learned about.  Tasks like renewal should be scheduled early enough that there's time to retry a few times (spaced apart by days, ideally) before the matter requires human attention and/or urgency.

Having said that, if we think we can catch cases like this and suggest the user try again, I'm open to it.

I fixed the issue of not being able to log back in immediately after a logout. It turns out that because we weren't creating a copy of the `DEFAULT_HEADERS` global object in `api/dispatch.js`, after the token had been set once it was being persisted until a page refresh. I fixed this by merging the `DEFAULT_HEADERS` object with the user-defined headers to create a fresh object every time.

However, I noticed another bug that I think is worth fixing. If a logout fails, the user is still directed to the login page and the logout page is never shown. After a bit of thought, I came to the conclusion that it doesn't really make sense to have `LogoutPage` at all. Instead, I think that `unauthenticateUser` should be passed as an action to `MainContainer`, which will then pass it onto `Header`. If a logout is successful, the user should be directed to the login page, and if not, an error message should be displayed. 

For an example of how to accomplish this with just actions, check out this code: https://github.com/ubyssey/dispatch/blob/logout/dispatch/static/manager/src/js/actions/UserActions.js#L35-L48

This tactic currently supports only `bvule` and `bvsle`, see [here](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/master/src/tactic/bv/bv_bounds_tactic.cpp#L194), so this example works:

```
(declare-fun a () (_ BitVec 8))
(assert (bvule #xff a))
(assert (bvule a #xfe))
(apply (then propagate-bv-bounds) :print_benchmark true :print false)
```

The `simplify` tactic will rewrite everything into `bvule`/`bvsle` form; just run it before running `bv-bounds-propagate`:

```
(apply (then simplify propagate-bv-bounds) :print_benchmark true :print false)
```

There's an important twist in the cuDNN batch normalization, by the way. Instead of computing the running average of the variance, they compute the running average of `1 / sqrt(variance + eps)`, misnamed as InvVariance:
```
/* Output in training mode, input in inference. Is the moving average
   of 1 / sqrt( epsilon + variance[x] ) */
void                               *resultRunningInvVariance,
```
We might want to change the batch normalization layer to do this as well, to make it easier to switch to the cuDNN version. That would finally give us a slight benefit for waiting so long with merging it, compared to all other libraries :)
This will have an effect on test-time inference (because our current `1 / sqrt(running_avg(variance) + eps)` is not the same as `running_avg(1 / sqrt(variance + eps))`), but probably it's neither for the worse nor for the better.

I'm not a fan of this personally, it causes interactions between weight initialization, learning rates, dropout probabilities etc. to be much more complicated, so parameter tuning becomes harder. We have  also discussed this in the past and decided to scale at train time (see #10).

If this is significantly affecting performance we could reconsider, but we'd need to see some (reproducible) benchmarks first :)

Does anyone have this working in a many to many context, in particular a model with an m2m relationship with itself?

For example, I have a Product model, with an m2m relationship to itself to represent products that are competitive with each other. Example data:

> product_name  |  competes_with
> Product A         | Product C
> Product B         |
> Product C         | Product B, Product D

I need Product C to be created while importing row 1 and related to Product A and Product D to be created with importing row 3 and linked to Product C.

The below will create new values, but will only create an M2M relationship if the foreign key value already exists. In the above example: 
- C will be created, but not related to A
- B will be related to C on importing C
- D will be created, but not related to C

`class M2MCreateWithForeignKey(widgets.ManyToManyWidget):
	def __init__(self, model, separator=', ', field='pk', defaults=None, create=False):
		self.model = model
		self.separator = separator
		self.field = field
		self.defaults = defaults
		self.create = create

	def clean(self, value):
		values = filter(None, value.split(self.separator))

		if self.create:
			for v in values:
				self.model.objects.get_or_create(defaults=self.defaults, **{self.field:v})
			return super(M2MCreateWithForeignKey, self).clean(value)
		else:
			try:
				val = super(M2MCreateWithForeignKey, self).clean(value)
			except ObjectDoesNotExist:
				model_name = self.model.__name__
				error = "Imported data includes non-existant %s(s) (%s) "\
					"\nThis model does not support creating a new %s when importing data."\
					% (model_name, value, model_name)
				logger.error(error)
				return None
			else:
				return val`

To use Gymrat to nop out that instruction, all you'll have to do is write a new lifter and register it. There are some good examples in [the pyvex spotters test](https://github.com/angr/pyvex/blob/master/tests/test_spotter.py). For reference, here is the easiest to understand example:
```python
class Instruction_IMAGINARY(Instruction):
    bin_format = bin(0x45e1)[2:].zfill(16)
    name = 'IMAGINARY'

    def compute_result(self):
        a = self.constant(10, Type.int_27)
        b = self.constant(20, Type.int_27)
        a + b

class ImaginarySpotter(GymratLifter):
    instrs = [Instruction_IMAGINARY]

register(ImaginarySpotter, 'ARMEL')
```
You'll create a class for each instruction you want to implement and give them each a binary format `bin_format` and name. Then instead of having the `compute_result` function do something, you can nop out the instruction by replacing the body with `pass`. Then all you have to do is create a lifter class with the instrs and register it for your architecture. This way, when libVEX hits an instruction it can't decode, your lifter will be run, match the instruction, and lift it. Documentation for `bin_format` can be found [here](https://github.com/angr/pyvex/blob/master/pyvex/lift/util/instr_helper.py). Let me know you have any questions!

So the ConstantOperand was also an issue, but orthogonal to what I meant. What I mean is:

- `400520  push [rip+0x200782]`, conceptually, conveys "this instruction reads from an offset of 0x200782 from rip"
- `400520  push [0x600ca8]`, conceptually, conveys "this instruction reads from 0x600ca8"

While those are the same if the program happens to be loaded at 0x400000, they are very different things in general. The latter representation doesn't leave room for the user to understand that, when loaded at a different offset, the instruction will actually read from a different memory address.

As a side story, the instruction pointer and memory layout have been the two hardest things to get right during this project. Here's why:

I've written a nearly complete VEX 8051 lifter (`8051_toIR`) and disassembler (`py8051`), which I've tied in to Angr through simuvex and archinfo. This was a project in of itself (and I'd like to upstream it to you), but once I got most instructions implemented, I moved on to actually executing 8051-based VEX IR with Angr. This is where some problems started arising. Angr was written for modern, "desktop" operating systems, meaning they have virtual memory and are most likely 32 or 64 bits. Correspondingly, VEX and Valgrind were written the same way, which bleeds in to Angr. For more embedded, "bare metal" architectures like 8051, there are some peculiarities that show up.

The first is the memory aliasing problem. 8051 has three distinct address spaces: RAM (including SFRs), code, and XDATA (external RAM). All three address spaces start at address 0x00 and are accessed by different versions of mov (`mov`, `movc`, and `movx`). This is an issue as just using pure VEX Store and Load leads to there being no way to figure out which memory segment needs to be accessed just from an IRSB. My solution to this was to use a VEX CCall with a prototype of `addr i8051g_memory(addr, segment_t)` in order to rebase memory accesses. For example, `movx A, @DPTR` which roughly translates to
```
------ IMark(0x0, 1, 0) ------
dptr_addr = i8051g_memory{0x4c49a0}(16Uto32(0x82:I16), 0x0:I8):I32)
dptr_value = LDle:I16(dptr_addr)

PUT(0) = LDle:I8(i8051g_memory{0x4c49a0}(16Uto32(dptr_value), 0x3:I8):I32)
PUT(36) = 0x1:I32
```
now uses the CCalls to take address 0x82 (DPTR) to SFR_BASE+0x82 and address DPTR to XDATA_BASE+DPTR.

The problem with this approach is that now it is possible for memory load addresses to go beyond 16-bits as we are utilizing memory ranges to communicate memory access segments. This approach was an alternative to changing or adding VEX instructions to naturally handle this case, which could break the FFI and prevent upstreaming. VEX has a word size setting that now has to be extended to 32-bits, even though the instruction pointer or any individual address space can never go beyond 64kb in reality. So now memory accesses have to be rewritten and my instruction pointer in VEX guest state has to be 32-bits or asserts get triggered in VEX. This means even though my IP in hardware is 16-bits, I have to emulate it as 32-bits, but store/load it to memory (through a call or ret) using only 2 bytes to be accurate.

The other issue related to memory is that the 8051 has memory mapped IO and registers. This means a write to address 0x7 is also a write to the R7 register in the 0th bank. I handled these aliases using `state.inspect` by getting complete mediation on all register/memory writes/reads.

Overall it has been a headache fixing these issues, which mostly stem from VEX limitations and 8051's strange architecture.

@mikerob You can change the initial heartbeat_timeout in the connect() method, but not after the fact.

I suppose we need five properties:

1. `vehicle.connected` — read-only bool if we are a live or dead connection (dead following timeout or .close()`
1. `vehicle.link_established` — read-only bool if we have crossed the heartbeat_threshold. this is independent of `.connected` and meant as a warning under congested situations
1. `vehicle.heartbeat_timeout` — read-write integer of how long since last heartbeat we close connection (normally 30s)
1. `vehicle.heartbeat_threshold` — read-write integer of how long since last heartbeat we determine link lost (normally 5s)
1. `vehicle.last_heartbeat` — read-only time since last heartbeat

Happy to bikeshed any of these. These should be easy to get into DKPY2, but let's settle on a minimal, "good enough" solution so we can land these soon.

The background fill has an alpha of 0 because the generated images are being used as textures by OpenGL, which handles transparency itself (i.e. all shapes and text in my screenshots above are rendered as textures and are then drawn to the display buffer). In order for overlapping textures to be able to blend, they need to be RGBA. Using a background with full opacity results in everything having a square of the background fill colour around it:

![screen shot 2018-01-07 at 8 54 37 pm](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/18648066/34847045-759b1b58-f6f0-11e7-9908-14ee58351d81.png)

If I use `(255, 255, 255, 0)` as the background, the texture gets brighter than it should be if there's any transparency in the shape drawn on it. If set the 'RGB' values of the background are set to the values of the Brush used to draw on it, the opacity works as expected.

Maybe AGG isn't set up to handle transparency properly when drawing to a surface with an alpha channel, so it just uses the same logic as if it's drawing to one with full opacity (i.e blending the brush colour with the background colour)? On a surface with 0 opacity I'd expect it to draw with the unaltered given Brush/Pen colour but with the opacity given (with results like I got with my hacky numpy workaround), but I'm not sure how the logic would work for something being drawn on a surface with, say, half-transparency.

@domtallo: What I understand from your previous messages is that you have a setup which is currently working: you have a reverse proxy doing the SSL offload properly, the only remaining problem you have is that the kinto server itself thinks it's running on the 8888 whereas you would like it to be on the 443 (HTTPS) protocol.

The message from @natim is proposing a different solution to what you currently have in place (Currently you have a docker container listening to a specific port, and outside the docker a reverse proxy doing the SSL offloading, from what I understand). The setup you have seems to be working, except from the host and port parts.

In order to do this, you can configure the kinto server (with the kinto.ini file) telling it the correct host and port.

Here is the link to the documentation: http://kinto.readthedocs.io/en/latest/configuration/settings.html#scheme-host-and-port, in your case, it means that your `kinto.ini` file should have the following configuration:

```ini
kinto.http_scheme = https
kinto.http_host = kinto.yourserver.tld
```

Hope that helps

This proposal is different than the feature_selection module. One could indeed do the following:

- start with 10 features
- use FunctionFeaturizer to generate 10,000 total features (1000 per initial feature)
- reduce that back down to 10 features using the feature_selection methods in scikit learn

But that's not the proposal. The proposal is:
- Start with 1 of the 10 features
- Generate 1000 features as per before for that 1 feature
- Pick the best function of that 1000
- Repeat for the remaining features

In both cases, you'll end up with 10 features. The first way is less restricted in the way that it can pick features since it can pick two functions of the same feature. Actually, I am trying to avoid that behavior which is the reason for this proposal. It can be argued which is "better". If you really trust your data and feel your feature selection routine is robust, then doing it the first way is fine. But if you just want to find the best "version" of each starting feature, this method is more in-line with that.

>> From a ML perspective, it feels like you would only explicitly add a function feature if you had a prior reason for believing it was important. If you were just trying to find better performance through basis expansion, that could be done using scikit-learn as part of the training pipeline, or by using a kernel method

The kernel methods are limited in what they find. e.g., you do a polynomial kernel and you get all polynomials, but you don't get log or sin functions. Plus, not all ML methods are compatible with kernel methods.

Similarly sklearn has preprocessing like polynomial features but again it doesn't allow for arbitrary mathematical functions

A connection is an enhanced structure for items that can be paginated, enabling the following capabilities for the client:
* The ability to paginate through the list.
* The ability to ask for information about the connection itself, like totalCount or pageInfo.
* The ability to ask for information about the edge itself, like cursor or friendshipTime.
* The ability to change how our backend does pagination since the user just uses opaque cursors.

@ohcibi 
> I simply want to login with credentials and receive the access token as a response in the client to authorize further requests with.

That shouldn't be a problem. The main question is _where do you want the user to enter the credentials_? As I see it you can use either the Client Authorization OAuth2 flow or the Resource Owner OAuth2 flow , depending on whether the user should be redirected to a login screen on the OAuth2 server or whether he should enter his credentials directly into the client. The Client Authorization flow is obviously more secure, whereas the Resource Owner flow is literally "post username/password to /token/ and receive the access token in response". `skip_authorization` is that you want for "client_ids" (Applications) that you trust. 

> Do I even need to extend the application model for it or is that field actually already present in the base class?

The [default Application model](https://github.com/jazzband/django-oauth-toolkit/blob/b4378b3d3197c4fb7b13b0f7ba624616f4241f28/oauth2_provider/models.py#L78) has `skip_authorization` built in. But you can replace the Application model with your own (it's `swappable` which is currently an undocumented internal API in Django). An example for how to use your own Application model can be found [on my `authserver` project](https://github.com/jdelic/authserver/blob/561c245b8680f546a310109b312532939a703dab/authserver/mailauth/models.py#L371) (also see `authserver.settings` and the associated initial migration on `mailauth`).

> Does that mean I have to preseed the database with an application record with that field set to true for a trusted application?

Yes, you have to create entries for your clients _and_ distribute their secrets to the clients. I build infrastructure in `authserver` to do this in the form of [custom management commands](https://github.com/jdelic/authserver/blob/561c245b8680f546a310109b312532939a703dab/authserver/mailauth/management/commands/oauth2.py#L37) which allow you to create applications from a script on the OAuth2 server and then publish the client secret to [Hashicorp Vault](https://vaultproject.io) (or Consul), where clients can then retrieve their secret from using `consul-template` or direct API access. Obviously nothing keeps you from just copy/pasting the secret :).

You're absolutely right but this is cloudformation behavior. If a stack fails to create by accident or user interaction, cloudformation executes a rollback meaning to delete all previously created resources. The stack itself stays in place in a rollback_completed state to enable users to inspect and debug the problems. It is not possible to create this stack again. Users have to delete the stack on their own. The error you get is what cloudformation api returns and nothing in scope of cfn-sphere. Cfn-sphere could maybe detect this state and call delete_stack prior to the create_stack call but this might not be the best solution for anybody. Furthermore this state is hard to detect since it is simply rollback_completed.

## Combining lines and planes (text from https://earthref.org/MagIC/books/Tauxe/Essentials/WebBook3ap3.html#x22-240000C.2.2)

- Calculate M directed lines (two in our case) and N great circles (one in our case) using principal component analysis (see Chapter 9) or Fisher statistics.
- Assume that the primary direction of magnetization for the samples with great circles lies somewhere along the great circle path (i.e., within the plane).
- Assume that the set of M directed lines and N unknown directions are drawn from a Fisher distribution.
- Iteratively search along the great circle paths for directions that maximize the resultant vector R for the M + N directions.
- Having found the set of N directions that lie along their respective great circles, estimate the mean direction using Equation 11.7 and κ as:
![webbook3514x](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/4332322/19630076/e35c88f6-9937-11e6-85f6-c0743b7ab991.png)
     
The cone of 95% confidence about the mean is given by:
![webbook3515x](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/4332322/19630086/087368da-9938-11e6-9820-7054fbeb1a1c.png)
where N′ = M + N∕2 and p = .02

Yeah, the removal of dependency on libs is to do with not needing them for simply running `setup.py`. The ideal is that `setup.py` should always run irrespective of whether or not the dependencies are installed or not.

It's also worth thinking about how this interacts with #176.

The issues with multiple lib versions goes pretty deep - there is a look up dict that references particular FFTW versions in the cython file. This would obviously be wrong in the case where an fftw version is missing. I think we can use conditional Cython statements based on the introspected variables you've discussed to solve this, but it needs a bit of care to get right.

This has been resolved. I removed the "has PR" label because I wanted to investigate and test before closing the issue. In the form described above, this issue was resolved by #814. In a more extreme case, the error is still possible due to #1456, but its enough of a pathological case that I feel comfortable closing this issue.

To provide more details, what occurred when this issue was written was the Nginx plugin raised an exception due to #921, however, due to issue #395, the client didn't rollback changes it made to your configuration. This is the piece that was resolved by #814. The client now catches all exceptions and most fatal signals to roll back your configuration before exiting, avoiding this issue.

Why wouldn't the client rollback your configuration? Great question Brad. When this issue was written, this always happened if the plugin had a problem, but now it could happen if the client raised an exception while rolling back your config (there's no known issue) or a signal like SIGINT or SIGKILL interrupted the error handler. While this later case is certainly possible, there is only so much we can do to stop the user from shooting themselves in the foot. Based on recent discussion with Peter, I opened #3215 to look into this issue.

With all that said, suppose the client failed to rollback your configuration. In this case, the client has a backup that it failed to restore saved on disk. The next time the client runs, it will attempt to restore this backup. If the user makes changes to their config before running the client again, however, these changes will be lost as described in #1456. I made #3216 to track the general issue of whether or not we should automatically attempt to restore backups if we previously failed to do so.

Despite just describing scenarios where the problem identified here can still happen, it only occurs in pathological cases and the problem is resolved when following the steps outlined in the original post. I'm closing this issue in favor of #3215 and #3216 which specifically address the pathological cases so they can be discussed and resolved on a more general level.

Sorry for changing our position on this, but since you've identified all places we use text wrapping in order to write this PR, implemented the new solution should be pretty easy if you're still interested.

The main problem identified in #2754 is we break things like URLs that would normally be clickable in a terminal. `textwrap` has options [break_long_words](https://docs.python.org/2/library/textwrap.html#textwrap.TextWrapper.break_long_words) and [break_on_hyphens](https://docs.python.org/2/library/textwrap.html#textwrap.TextWrapper.break_on_hyphens) which solve this problem. Using the examples, provided [here](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/issues/2754#issue-145527636), output becomes:

```
Congratulations! You have successfully enabled
https://02e645.le-test.hoffman-andrews.com

You should test your configuration at:
https://www.ssllabs.com/ssltest/analyze.html?d=02e645.le-test.hoffman-andrews.com
```
which solves the problem and still allows for text wrap to make the output look a little better.

Ok, encryption depends on `func.__refcalls__`. If I set it initially to `1` the bytecode is never encrypted again, right?

`func.__refcalls__` must be 0 initially. And `__wraparmor__(func, tb, 1)` must match `__wraparmor__(func)`.
Otherwise, pyarmor will think the function is running, so does nothing.
Call `__wraparmor__(func)` will increase `func.__refcalls`, and `__wraparmor__(func, tb, 1)` decrease.
Only `func.__refcalls__` equals 1, pyarmor goes into action.

# Units of scored values in SHIELD-HIT12A

Detector types IDET(5,*):
-  0: NONE [nil]
-  1: ENERGY [MeV/primary]
-  2: FLUENCE [cm^{-2}/primary]
-  3: CROSSFLU [cm^{-2}/primary]
-  4: LETFLU 
-  5: DOSE [MeV/g/primary] in case of *MSH*/*CYL*   ZONE currently only [MeV/primary]
-  6: DLET [MeV/cm]
-  7: TLET [MeV/cm]
-  8: AVG-ENERGY [MeV]
-  9: AVG-BETA [dimensionless]
- 10: SPC [has no .bdo file output]
- 11: MATERIAL [dimensionless]
- 12: DDD [not implemented]
- 13: ALANINE [MeV/g/primary] in case of *MSH*/*CYL*   ZONE currently only [MeV/primary]
- 14: NORMALIZED COUNTER [/primary]
- 15: PET ISOTOPE COUNTER [/primary]
- 16: DLETG  [MeV/cm]
- 17: TLETG  [MeV/cm] 
- 18: ZONE# [dimensionless]
- 19: MEDIUM# [dimensionless]
- 20: RHO [g/cm^3]

IDET(1),IDET(2),IDET(3),IDET(4):
- MSH : [cm],[cm],[cm], (nil)
- DMSH : [cm],[cm],[cm],[#/MeV]
- CYL : [cm],[cm],[radians],(nil)
- DCYL : [cm],[cm],[radians],[#/MeV]
- DMSHZ/DCYLZ have no .bdo output
- ZONE: [zone number]
- VOXSCORE : [cm],[cm],[cm] but in CT coordinate system
- GEOMAP: [cm],[cm],[cm] (as MSH)
- PLANE: (none)

Note that when you compile Ray, cmake will look for Python libraries to use to build Ray's Python C extensions. It will find the Python libraries that are associated with the version of Python that is found when you run `which python` from the command line. So if you call `setup.py install` with a version of Python that is different from the one that `which python` finds, then you will probably encounter this problem. For example, this could happen if you do `python3 setup.py install`.

This problem is probably the same as #241.

Hi, @elrik75

I've do some research in psycopg2. And it seems that execute is synchronous. Results are also fetched immediately if PostgreSQL cursor isn't defined explicitly via DECLARE CURSOR and further FETCH.

Here is simple code:

```python
import psycopg2

conn = psycopg2.connect("dbname=test host=localhost user=test password=test")
cur = conn.cursor()
cur.execute("SELECT * FROM test_table")
raw_input('input')  # You can even stop postgresql server here. Results are already stored in python.
print(cur.fetchall())  # There will be no errors.
```

Query is sent and all data was fetched immediately after `.execute()`, so `.fetch*()` read data from internal buffer. I suppose it's the right thing to read data from socket as soon as possible and do not wait for `.fetch()` call. Otherwise not read data will be stalled in system's socket buffer. Let's sum up: all calls is blocking and data is fetched from socket immediately.

The same approach was used in this driver `.execute()` method: send query, skip non-data packets and read all data **at once**.

But here comes progress notification.

Obviously we should only send query and then allow user to read progress. Let it be `.fetchprogress()`. Yup, if user don't want to read progress `.fetchall()` can be used. But there is no way to read **current** progress info. After query is sent, server starts to send packet to client that should be read. All progress packets will be stored in socket buffer, until user call `.fetchprogress()` as many times progress packets were received.

Unfortunately conditional blocking (in terms of data/progress receiving) is the only right way that I see. 

Summary: queries in PG are read in atomic-way in `.execute()`. In ClickHouse in order to read progress we shouldn't use atomic reads, but it's no good for sockets.

What's you opinion?

# Stopgap measure design notes

I was thinking some more about a possible stopgap measure that we might be able to sneak into 3.7 to make something like #176 fast. Basically the idea would be to get just enough help from the interpreter to make checking for unawaited coroutines fast enough that we can afford to do it at every context switch.

(There was some previous discussion of these ideas in #176 starting here: https://github.com/python-trio/trio/pull/176#issuecomment-304490423)

In more detail, the requirements would be:

* At a "barrier" (= yield point or catch-all exception handler), reliably and quickly detect whether there are any unawaited coroutines created since the last barrier

* If so, get a list of them so we can give a nice error message

* Also, we want to be able to disable this checking on a per-task basis, because some tasks might be "hosting" asyncio code (#171). So we need to be able to "consume" the list of unawaited coroutines -- if one task creates some and we think that's OK and leave them be, then we don't want to re-detect those coroutines the next time we check.

There are two fairly natural ways to do this that come to mind. They both involve the same basic strategy: adding two pointers to each coroutine object, which we can use to create a double-linked list holding the coroutines of interest (with the list head stored in the threadstate). The nice thing about an intrusive double-linked list like this is that it's a collection where adding and removing are both O(1) and very cheap (just updating a few pointers).

## API option 1

Keep a thread-local list of live, unawaited coroutines. So coroutines always insert themselves into the list when created, and then remove themselves again when they're either iterated or deallocated.

Then we need:

* A way to get and clear the list of coroutines: `sys.get_and_clear_unawaited_coroutines()`

* A way to register a hook that's called whenever an unawaited coroutine is garbage collected: `sys.set_unawaited_coroutine_gc_hook`, `sys.get_unawaited_coroutine_gc_hook`

```python
def unawaited_coroutine_gc_hook(coro):
    _gced_unawaited_coros.add(coro)

def barrier():
    live_unawaited_coros = sys.get_and_clear_unawaited_coros()
    if _gced_unawaited_coros or live_unawaited_coros:
        # do expensive stuff here, checking if this task is hosting asyncio, etc.
        ...
```

(We need to have both hooks to handle the two cases of (a) a coroutine is created and then immediately garbage collected in between barriers, (b) a coroutine is created and then still live when we hit the barrier.)

For speed, we might want to also have a `sys.has_unawaited_coros()` that returns a bool, so that we don't need to bother calling `get_and_clear_unawaited_coros` every time and pay the price of allocating an empty list object just to throw it away. `%timeit bool([])` says 127 ns and `%timeit get_coroutine_wrapper()` (used here as a proxy for a trivial function that just returns a value from the threadstate) says 37 ns, so it's faster but they're both fast enough I don't know whether it matters without some kind of testing.

A minor awkward question here is whether the regular unawaited coro warning would be issued at the same time our hook is called, or whether we'd have some way to suppress it, or...

Oh wait, there's also a more worrisome problem: if we see a coroutine is unawaited and live, and decide that it's OK... what happens if it later gets garbage collected and is still unawaited? We'll see it again, but now in some random other context. I guess what we could do is that when we detect live unawaited coroutines and we're in asyncio-friendly mode, we put them into a `WeakSet` (or even a `WeakDict` to track which task created them), and then our gc hook checks to see if they're in the `WeakSet`. Except... I think `__del__` might run after weakrefs are cleared? If so then this whole approach is probably sunk. Or maybe we could somehow mark the coroutines that we've seen before? You can't attach arbitrary attributes to coroutines. I suppose we could do some nasty hack involving the coroutine's locals... those should still be accessible from `__del__`.

## API option 2

In the design above, the need to handle GCed unawaited coroutines is the cause of lots of different kinds of awkwardness. What we could do instead is make our tracking list a *strong* reference, so that coroutines insert themselves when created, and then stay there either until they're iterated or else we remove them manually.

Obviously this can't be enabled by default, because it would mean that instead of warning about unawaited coroutines, the interpreter would just leak them. So the API would be something like:

`sys.enable_unawaited_coroutine_tracking()`, `sys.disable_unawaited_coroutine_tracking()`, `sys.get_and_clear_unawaited_coroutines()`

And then everything else is pretty straightforward and obvious. (Though we still have the question about whether we'd want a `sys.has_unawaited_coroutines()` for speed.)

## PyPy's opinion

I asked @arigo about how these options look from the point of view of PyPy. He said that if `sys.get_and_clear_unawaited_coros()` was written carefully, then it would probably be possible for the JIT to inline and optimize out the unnecessary empty list, so that's a useful data point. (Though I guess it might still be better to save it the trouble of needing to, esp. since you're not always in JIT mode.)

He also had a strong preference for the second API on the general grounds that doing anything from `__del__` methods is just asking for trouble and creates all kinds of problems for alternative interpreter implementations. Obviously they support it, but it's not cheap. In this particular case it's not a huge practical difference b/c coroutines already have a `__del__` method (the one that prints the `Coroutine '...' was never awaited` message, or potentially throws `GeneratorExit`). But he felt that option 2 was just simple from an abstract "let's try to make it easier rather than harder to write a python implementation" perspective.

## Other possible consumers

Nick points out that this kind of list structure might also be useful for more general introspection, similar to `threading.enumerate()`: https://github.com/python-trio/trio/pull/176#issuecomment-304492602

Curio, possibly. Not sure, would have to check with Dave.

Coming back around to this again, I'd like to make a more refined proposal:

* A fixture is a "trio fixture" if any of the following are true:

  * created with `@trio_fixture` (required for projects that want to use multiple `pytest-(.*)io` packages)

  * it's an async fixture *and* the "everything async is trio" configuration flag is enabled (#9)

  * it's a fixture that depends on a "trio fixture"

* It's an error to use a "trio fixture" in any situation except (a) test scope, (b) the test is marked `@pytest.mark.trio`

* All "trio fixtures" get the special delayed handling when combined with the `@pytest.mark.trio` runner

I think this provides a good balance of functionality for both values of the "everything is trio" config flag, should provide useful error messages when misused, and has at least the potential to play nicely with packages like `pytest-asyncio` and `pytest-curio`.

Of course, right now, any system that allows for plain `@pytest.fixture` to work on regular async functions will be broken if `pytest-asyncio` is installed, because that's how it works, and that includes this proposal for when "everything async is trio" mode is enabled. But there would be path forward at least, since in this proposal that brokenness would be entirely on pytest-asyncio's side, and we could hopefully convince them to implement a similar setup. (@Tinche: sound plausible?)

1. the sources are added to the target directory

@sergeigofman 
1. We understand self-contained operator might lead to better performance in some specific architectures. However, I think quantization itself is not stable enough to be standardized at this moment. Hope you can understand it. If users really care about performance, as @Ke mentioned, there is another proposed op called Function #802, which provides a hint to contain a sequence of multiple operators. That might help for some vendors.

2a. I will be surprised that it will cause overflow. 
When x_int is int8 and x_zero is also int8, (x_int - x_zero) using Sub op should stay in int8 range, if 0.0f is in the dynamic range of data or near it. If not, that simply means your dynamic range is far to 0.0f. In those cases, don't you think you pick a wrong quantization formula. Switch to the formula in 1, it should work well. If you really want to use this formula in this case, you can add a Cast op to promote it from int8 to int32 to avoid overflow. Then you can add Function to force self-containing if you have a concern of API mapping in your platform.

2b is the case that x_zero is float. There are tons of quantization methods. You never know what users can come up with. :)

This question, like Francisco said, is related to `httplib`, once it doesn't look for proxy configs in your system. It could be solved with [`urllib2`](http://docs.python.org/library/urllib2) easily, I haven't done this yet because this would cause an interface change in splinter, we would have to remove the `status_code` property from the webdrivers, which was implemented solving [issue 35](https://github.com/cobrateam/splinter/issues/35). The reason why we can't keep it, is that [`urllib2`](http://docs.python.org/library/urllib2) does not give us access to http status code when we make a request, only when it raises an [HTTPError](http://docs.python.org/library/urllib2#urllib2.HTTPError).

The `status_code` support was not the goal of [issue 35](https://github.com/cobrateam/splinter/issues/35), it's a gain.

I'm bringing this up because the `status_code` is part of the webdriver's api, and remove it will break people who are using it.

Thanks for tracking that down!  Your analysis is correct that the free-function is called from the IO thread, and that's causing the problem.  This is unavoidable[1] for non-copying sends, but fortunately it *only* affects non-copying sends, and for that matter, only affects non-copying sends that will be discarded at termination due to LINGER *and* failure to cleanup the context in your own code.  Understanding that, here's a simpler repro script:

```python
import zmq
ctx = zmq.Context.instance()
socket = ctx.socket(zmq.REQ)
socket.linger = 0
socket.connect('tcp://127.0.0.1:12345') # nothing listening
socket.send(b'abc', copy=False)

# explicit close&term prior to Python exit eliminates the issue
# ctx.destroy()

print("begin cleanup")
# if destroy was not called, this will crash in Python3
```

ZMQStream does non-copying sends by default for some reason, which is a bad choice, so I am going to revert that in PR #172.  For now, the answer is going to be: you must use explicit cleanup if you are doing non-copying sends and might discard them at shutdown due to LINGER.  In your sample script, that amounts to a simple `ctx.term` at the end to ensure clean shutdown.

I'm not sure this is a *huge* deal, because it only affects already exiting processes, but there have been other places where grabbing the GIL from the io_thread has caused weirdness.  

[1] The only solution I have in my head is for the free_fn to actually be called from *yet another* thread that pyzmq creates, and the io_thread's callback somehow GIL-lessly puts the necessary info in a queue and notifies (or spawns) the thread, which grabs the GIL and deletes the object.  That should avoid this problem, because it doesn't really matter that the free_fn is aborted at shutdown, it only matters that the io_thread is killed, which will not happen if it never grabs the GIL.  But that means I would have to look up how to spawn and communicate with a C-thread in a platform-independent way, which isn't going to happen soon.
